2.1: My son, if you undertake to serve the Lord, prepare your soul for the test.
2.2: Straighten your heart and be steadfast, and do not rush headlong into times of misfortune.
2.3: Cling to God and do not separate yourself from Him, so that you may be fulfilled in your last days.
2.4: Accept whatever comes your way, and in the vicissitudes of your humiliation, be patient.
2.5: For gold is tested in the fire, and men pleasing to God are tested in the crucible of humiliation.
2.6: Have faith in God and he will lift you up, make your ways straight and hope in him.
2.7: You who fear the Lord, wait for his mercy and do not turn away, lest you fall.
2.8: You who fear the Lord, have faith in him and your reward will not be lost.
2.9: You who fear the Lord, hope for happiness, eternal joy, and mercy.
2.10: Consider the ancient generations and see: who ever hoped in the Lord and was put to shame? Who remained faithful to his fear and was forsaken? Who called upon him and received from him only contempt?
2.11: For the Lord is compassionate and merciful; he forgives sins and delivers in the day of affliction.
2.12: Woe to the timid hearts, to the weak hands, to the sinner who walks in two ways.
2.13: Woe to the weak heart, because it does not have faith in God. Therefore, it will not be protected.
2.14: Woe to you who have lost patience! What will you do on the day of the Lord's visit?
2.15: Those who fear the Lord are not disobedient to his words, and those who love him faithfully keep his ways.
2.16: Those who fear the Lord seek his good pleasure, and those who love him are filled with his law.
2.17: Those who fear the Lord prepare their hearts and humble their souls before him.,
2.18: saying: We will fall into the hands of the Lord and not into the hands of men, for as much as he has power, so much does he have mercy.

3.1: My children, listen to me, your father, and ensure that you obtain salvation.
3.2: For the Lord wants the father to be honored by his children, and he has established the authority of the mother over the sons.
3.3: He who honors his father atones for his sins.
3.4: And honoring one's mother is like amassing a treasure.
3.5: He who honors his father will be gladdened by his children and will be heard on the day of his prayer.
3.6: He who honors his father will have long days, he who obeys the Lord will give comfort to his mother.
3.7: [He who fears the Lord honors his parents] and serves as masters those who gave him birth.
3.8: Honor your father in word and deed, so that his blessing may come upon you.,
3.9: for the blessing of the father strengthens the homes of children, but the curse of the mother uproots the foundations.
3.10: Do not boast of your father's disgrace, for his shame cannot bring you honor.
3.11: for a man's glory comes from the honor of his father, and a despised mother is the shame of her children.
3.12: My son, support your father in his old age and do not grieve him during his life.
3.13: Should his spirit weaken, be lenient and do not despise him in the fullness of your strength.
3.14: For good deeds done to a father will not be forgotten, and in place of your sins your house will prosper.
3.15: On the day of tribulation, the Lord will remember you., As ice melts in calm weather, so your sins will disappear.
3.16: He who forsakes his father is like a blasphemer; he who provokes his mother is cursed by the Lord.
3.17: My son, perform your works with gentleness and you will be loved by the man pleasing to God.
3.18: Humble yourself all the more because you are greater, and you will find favor in the sight of the Lord.,
3.19: for the power of the Lord is great, and he is glorified by the humble.
3.20: Don't seek what is too difficult for you, and don't scrutinize what is beyond your strength.
3.21: What you are commanded to do, that is what you must think about, for you have no use for hidden things.
3.22: Do not apply yourself to superfluous occupations, for you have already been shown more than the human mind can comprehend.
3.23: Their own illusion has misled many, and a guilty presumption has diverted their thoughts.
3.24: He who loves danger will find his downfall, and the hard-hearted will ultimately fall into misfortune.
3.25: The hard-hearted will be overwhelmed with afflictions, and the sinner piles sin upon sin.
3.26: The misfortune of the proud is beyond remedy, for the plant of evil has taken root within him.
3.27: The heart of the intelligent man meditates on the parable; finding a listening ear is the desire of the wise.
3.28: Water extinguishes the fiercest fire, and almsgiving atones for sins.
3.29: He who repays kindness thinks of the future, and in the day of misfortune, he will find support.

4.1: My son, do not deprive the poor of their sustenance, do not make the eyes of the needy wait.
4.2: Do not afflict the soul of the hungry, nor embitter the poor man in his distress.
4.3: It does not further irritate an exasperated heart and does not defer to giving to the needy.
4.4: Do not reject the suffering supplicant, and do not turn your face away from the poor.
4.5: Do not turn your gaze away from the needy and do not give them an opportunity to curse you.,
4.6: For if he curses you in the bitterness of his soul, the one who made him will hear his prayer.
4.7: Make yourself agreeable to society and lower your head before a great man.
4.8: Lend an ear to the poor and gently give them a reply that will gladden them.
4.9: Rescue the oppressed from the hands of the oppressor and do not be cowardly when you administer justice.
4.10: Be like a father to orphans, like a husband to their mother, and you will be like a son of the Most High, who will love you more than your mother.
4.11: Wisdom exalts her children and takes care of those who seek her.
4.12: Those who love it love life, and those who earnestly seek it are filled with joy.
4.13: Whoever grasps it will have glory as his portion, and wherever he enters, the Lord will bless him.
4.14: Those who serve her serve the Holy One, and those who love her are loved by the Lord.
4.15: He who listens to her will judge the nations, and he who comes to her will dwell in safety.
4.16: He who puts his trust in it will have it as his inheritance, and his descendants will retain possession of it.
4.17: For, at first, she embarks with him on a difficult path, she brings fear and dread upon him, she torments him with her harsh discipline, until she can trust him and has tested him with her prescriptions.
4.18: But then, she returns to him by the right path, she delights him and reveals her secrets to him.
4.19: If he goes astray, she abandons him and leaves him to his doom.
4.20: Observe the weather and guard yourself against evil, and do not have to be ashamed of yourself.
4.21: There is a shame that leads to sin and there is a shame that attracts glory and grace.
4.22: Show no regard to anyone to the detriment of your soul, and do not be ashamed of your loss.
4.23: Do not withhold a word in the time of salvation, [and do not hide your wisdom through vain glory],
4.24: for it is by language that wisdom is recognized, and by the words of the tongue that knowledge is shown.
4.25: Do not contradict the truth, but be ashamed of your lack of education.
4.26: Do not be ashamed to confess your sins and do not fight against the current of the river.
4.27: Do not submit to a foolish man, and do not show respect to the person of a powerful man.
4.28: Fight for the truth until death, and the Lord God will fight for you.
4.29: Don't be harsh in your speech, lazy and cowardly in your actions.
4.30: Do not be like a lion in your house, nor capricious among your servants.
4.31: Do not let your hand be outstretched to receive and drawn back to give.

5.1: Do not rely on your wealth and do not say, "I have enough."«
5.2: Do not follow your desires and your strength to satisfy the desires of your heart
5.3: And do not say, "Who will be my master?" For surely the Lord will punish you.
5.4: Do not say, «I have sinned, and what has happened to me?» For the Lord is patient.
5.5: Do not be without fear concerning the atonement, lest you add sin upon sin.
5.6: Do not say, «God’s mercy is great; he will forgive my many sins.» For in him are mercy and anger, and his wrath falls upon sinners.
5.7: Do not delay in turning to the Lord and do not put it off from day to day, for the Lord’s anger will suddenly break out and, on the day of vengeance, you will perish.
5.8: Do not rely on unjust riches, for they will not avail you at all in the day of calamity.
5.9: Do not winnow in every wind and do not walk in every way, so does the double-tongued sinner.
5.10: Be firm in your feelings and let your language be consistent.
5.11: Be quick to listen, and slow to respond.
5.12: If you have intelligence, answer your neighbor; otherwise, put your hand over your mouth.
5.13: Glory and shame are in speech, and the tongue of man brings about his downfall.
5.14: Do not bring upon yourself the name of a slanderer and do not lay snares with your tongue, for shame falls upon the thief and severe condemnation befalls the double-tongued.
5.15: Be neither too much nor too little at fault,

6.1: and do not become an enemy from a friend, for the wicked will have in their portion bad name, shame and reproach: such is the double-tongued sinner.
6.2: Do not exalt yourself by the will of your soul, lest your soul be plundered, as by a bull.
6.3: You will devour your foliage, you will destroy your fruits, and you will leave nothing of yourself but a barren forest.
6.4: The perverse soul destroys the one who possesses it; it makes him the laughingstock of his enemies.
6.5: A kind word makes many friends, and a kind tongue is rich in kindnesses.
6.6: Many may be those who live in good relations with you, but take counsel from one in a thousand.
6.7: If you want to gain a friend, gain them by testing them and don't confide in them lightly.
6.8: For there is a friend at times like these who will not remain so in the day of your affliction.,
6.9: Such a friend will become an enemy and will reveal your disagreement to your shame.,
6.10: He is a friend when he sits at your table, but will not remain so on the day of your misfortune.
6.11: During your prosperity, he will be like another version of yourself and will speak freely with the people of your household.
6.12: If you fall into humiliation, he will be against you and hide from you.
6.13: Stay away from your enemies and be on your guard with your friends.
6.14: A loyal friend is a powerful protection; whoever finds one has found a treasure.
6.15: Nothing is more valuable than a loyal friend; no weight can express their worth.
6.16: A faithful friend is a remedy for life; those who fear the Lord find him.
6.17: He who fears the Lord has true friendship, for his friend is like him.
6.18: My son, from your youth devote yourself to learning, and until your hair turns gray you will find wisdom.
6.19: Approach it like the plowman and the sower, and wait for its good fruits. For a little while you will have difficulty cultivating it, but soon you will eat its fruits.
6.20: How steep it is for the ignorant. The fool will not cling to it.
6.21: Like a heavy stone of trial, it weighs upon him and he soon rejects it.
6.22: For wisdom justifies its name: it is not revealed to the masses.
6.23: Listen, my son, and receive my thoughts and do not reject my advice.
6.24: Engage your foot in its shackles and your neck in its collar.
6.25: Bend your shoulder to carry it and don't get irritated by its ties.
6.26: Come to her with all your soul and keep her ways with all your strength.
6.27: Follow her tracks and seek her out, and she will reveal herself to you; and when you have grasped her, do not let her go.
6.28: For in the end you will find her rest and she will become a source of joy for you.
6.29: Her shackles will become a powerful protection for you, and her collar a garment of glory.
6.30: For on her head is an ornament of gold and her headbands are of hyacinth fabric.
6.31: You will put it on as a robe of glory and you will place it on your head as a crown of joy.
6.32: My son, if you wish, you will acquire knowledge and, if you apply your soul, you will become skilled.
6.33: If you like to listen, you will learn; if you pay attention, you will become wise.
6.34: Stay in the company of old men; is there anyone who is wise? Attach yourself to him.
6.35: Listen willingly to any discourse about God and do not let the maxims of wisdom escape you.
6.36: If you see a man of sense, be near him from the morning and let your foot wear down the threshold of his door.
6.37: Meditate on the Lord’s commandments and constantly reflect on his precepts; he himself will strengthen your heart and the wisdom you desire will be given to you.

7.1: Do not do evil, and evil will not overtake you.,
7.2: Stay away from what is unjust and the unjust will stay away from you.
7.3: My son, do not sow in the furrows of injustice, if you do not want to reap seven times as much.
7.4: Do not ask the Lord for power, nor the king for a seat of honor.
7.5: Do not try to appear righteous before the Lord, and do not seek to appear wise before the king.
7.6: Do not seek to become a judge, lest you lack the strength to eradicate injustice, lest you be intimidated in the presence of a powerful man and jeopardize your fairness.
7.7: Don't offend the entire population of a city and don't throw yourself into the middle of a crowd.
7.8: Do not sin twice, for even for one sin you will not go unpunished.
7.9: Do not say, «God will consider the multitude of my gifts, and when I offer them to the Most High God, he will accept them.»
7.10: Do not be weak in your prayer and do not neglect to give alms.
7.11: Do not mock the man whose heart is afflicted, for there is one who humbles and one who exalts.
7.12: Do not fabricate lies against your brother, nor do such a thing against your friend.
7.13: Beware of telling any lies, because continuous lying does not turn out well.
7.14: Do not chatter in the company of old people and do not repeat the words of your prayer.
7.15: Do not hate hard labor, nor the work in the fields instituted by the Most High.
7.16: Do not place yourself among the majority of sinners; remember that vengeance will not be long in coming.
7.17: Humble your soul deeply, for fire and worm are the punishment of the wicked.
7.18: Do not exchange a friend for money, nor a brother for the gold of Ophir.
7.19: Do not turn away from a wise and good wife, for her charm is better than gold.
7.20: Do not mistreat the faithfully working slave, nor the employee who devotes himself to your service.
7.21: Love the intelligent slave, do not deprive him of his freedom.
7.22: If you have flocks, take care of them and, if they are useful to you, keep them at home.
7.23: If you have sons, instruct them and subdue them from their childhood.
7.24: If you have daughters, ensure their chastity and do not have a jovial face with them.
7.25: Marry your daughter and you will have finished a big deal and give her to an intelligent man.
7.26: If you have a wife after your own heart, do not divorce her, [but do not give yourself to a woman who is contrary to you].
7.27: Honor your father with all your heart and do not forget your mother's pain.
7.28: Remember that it is through them that you came into the world: and how will you repay them for what they have given you?
7.29: Fear the Lord with all your soul and hold his priests in high esteem.
7.30: Love with all your strength the One who made you and do not neglect his ministers.
7.31: Fear the Lord and honor the priest, give him his portion, as it was prescribed from the beginning: the victim for the crime with the offering of the shoulders, the holy oblation and the first fruits.
7.32: Extend your hand to the poor as well, so that your blessing may be complete.
7.33: Give freely to every living person and do not withhold your favor from the dead.
7.34: Do not fail those who mourn, and grieve with those who grieve.
7.35: Do not neglect to take care of the sick; for such acts of charity you will be loved by God.
7.36: In all your actions remember your end, and you will never sin.

8.1: Do not argue with a powerful man, lest you fall into his hands.
8.2: Do not quarrel with a rich man, lest he may use the weight of his gold against you, for gold has ruined many, and has even turned the hearts of kings away.
8.3: Don't argue with a loudmouth and don't pile wood on his fire.
8.4: Do not joke with an ill-mannered man, lest your ancestors be dishonored.
8.5: Do not mock the man who turns away from sin; remember that we are all worthy of punishment.
8.6: Do not despise a man in his old age, for some of us are getting old too.
8.7: Do not rejoice when a man dies, remember that we will all die.
8.8: Do not neglect the speeches of the wise and dwell on their maxims, for you will learn from them instruction and the art of serving the great.
8.9: Do not ignore the words of the elders, for they themselves learned from their fathers. You will learn wisdom from them and how to answer well when the time comes.
8.10: Do not kindle the coals of the sinner, lest you be consumed by the heat of his flame.
8.11: Do not stand up to the insolent person who insults you, lest he lie in wait to spy on your words.
8.12: Do not lend to anyone more powerful than yourself, and if you have lent him something, consider it lost.
8.13: Don't act as guarantor beyond your means, and if you have acted as guarantor, worry as if you were going to pay.
8.14: Don't have a dispute with a judge because the decision will be made for him based on his opinion.
8.15: Do not travel with a reckless man, lest he become a burden to you, for he does everything according to his fancy and, by his folly, you will perish with him.
8.16: Do not quarrel with an irascible man and do not cross the desert with him, for blood is as nothing in his eyes, and where there is no help, he will strike you down.
8.17: Do not take advice from a madman, for he will be unable to keep anything secret.
8.18: Do nothing in front of a stranger that should remain hidden, because you don't know what he might do.
8.19: Do not reveal your heart to every man, if you do not want to be badly rewarded.

9.1: Do not be jealous of the woman who rests on your breast and do not awaken in her mind, to your detriment, an evil idea.
9.2: Do not surrender your soul to your wife, so that she rises up against your authority.
9.3: Do not go to meet a courtesan, for fear of falling into her trap.
9.4: Don't stay too long with a singer, lest you be captivated by her art.
9.5: Do not fix your gaze on a young girl, for fear of having to suffer punishment because of her.
9.6: Do not give yourself to courtesans, lest you lose your inheritance.
9.7: Do not let your eyes wander the streets of the city and do not prowl in its lonely places.
9.8: Avert your eyes from the elegant woman and do not gaze curiously at a foreign beauty. Many are seduced by a woman's beauty, and passion ignites within them like a fire.
9.9: Never sit beside a married woman, [do not lean on her elbows at the table], and do not drink wine with her at banquets, lest your soul turn to her and passion lead you to your ruin.
9.10: Don't abandon an old friend, for a new one won't be worth it. New wine, new friend; let it age and you'll drink it with pleasure.
9.11: Do not envy the glory of the sinner, for you do not know what his ruin will be.
9.12: Do not rejoice with the wicked in their success; remember that they will not go unpunished until they reach Sheol.
9.13: Stay away from the man who has the power to kill, and you will not fear death. And if you approach him, beware of all sin, lest he take your life. Know that you are walking among snares and that you are walking on the battlements of the city.
9.14: Observe your neighbor as much as possible and take advice from the wise.
9.15: Converse with intelligent men, and let all your conversations be based on the law of the Most High.
9.16: Let righteous men be those with whom you eat, and let your glory be in the fear of God.
9.17: It is the hand of the artist that earns a work praise, it is his word that makes the leader of the people appear wise.
9.18: The loudmouth is feared in his city, and the reckless one attracts hatred with his speeches.

10.1: The wise prince keeps his people disciplined, and the government of the sensible man is well-ordered.
10.2: Like the leader of the people, like his ministers, and like the government of the city, like all its inhabitants.
10.3: An ignorant king loses his people, but a city prospers through the intelligence of its leaders.
10.4: The sovereignty of the country is in the hands of the Lord, and he raises up for it in his time a suitable prince.
10.5: A man's success is in the hand of the Lord; it is He who places His authority on the brow of the leader.
10.6: Do not hold a grudge against your neighbor for any injustice, and do nothing amidst acts of violence.
10.7: Pride is hateful to God and to men, and for both, injustice is an iniquity.
10.8: Domination passes from one people to another because of injustice, insults, and the lust for wealth.
10.9: Why does that which is dust and earth boast? For during its life I put turmoil in its members,
10.10: The illness is long, the doctor laughs, and today's king will die tomorrow.
10.11: And when man dies, he has the larvae, the beasts, and the worms as his inheritance.
10.12: Pride begins when man separates himself from the Lord and when the heart turns away from the One who made him.
10.13: For the beginning of pride is sin, and whoever clings to it spreads abominations like rain. Therefore the Lord sends out marvelous punishments and strikes the wicked with utter destruction.
10.14: The Lord overthrows the throne of princes and places gentle men in their place.
10.15: The Lord uproots the nations and plants the humble in their place.
10.16: The Lord overthrows the lands of the nations and pursues their destruction to the foundations of the earth.
10.17: It dries up many of them and exterminates their inhabitants, erasing their memory from the earth.
10.18: Pride is not made for man, nor insolent anger for those born of woman.
10.19: Which race is honored? The race of man. Which race is honored? Those who fear the Lord. Which race is despised? The race of man. Which race is despised? Those who transgress the commandments.
10.20: In the midst of his brothers, their leader is honored, and those who fear the Lord are likewise honored in his sight.
10.21: Rich, noble and poor, their glory is the fear of the Lord.
10.22: It is not right to despise a poor intelligent person; it is not fitting to honor a sinner.
10.23: The great, the judge, and the powerful are honored, but none of them is greater than the one who fears the Lord.
10.24: Free men are servants of the prudent slave, and the intelligent man will not murmur.
10.25: Do not reason in order to accomplish your work, and do not boast in the time of your distress.
10.26: Better is the man who works hard in all things than the one who walks about, boasts, and lacks bread.
10.27: My son, honor your soul with gentleness and give it the respect it deserves.
10.28: Who will consider a man righteous if he sins against his own soul? Who will honor him who dishonors his life?
10.29: The poor are honored for their knowledge and the rich are honored for their wealth.
10.30: But how much more honored is someone who is honored in poverty? And how much more honored is someone who is honored in wealth?

11.1: The wisdom of the humble man will raise his head and make him sit in the midst of the great.
11.2: Do not praise a man for his beauty, and do not disgust a man for his appearance.
11.3: The bee is small among birds and its product ranks first among sweet things.
11.4: Do not boast about the clothes you wear, nor exalt yourself on the day of glory. For the works of the Lord are wonderful, and his deeds among men are hidden.
11.5: Many princes sat on the cobblestones, and the one no one expected wore the crown.
11.6: Many powerful people have been burdened with disgrace, and illustrious men delivered into the hands of others.
11.7: Before you seek information, don't cast blame, examine first and then you can proceed.
11.8: Do not respond before listening and do not throw your words into the middle of the speeches of others.
11.9: Do not argue about something that is not yours, and do not sit with sinners to judge.
11.10: My son, do not apply your energy to a multitude of things: if you take on too much, you will not be free from error. If you pursue too many things, you will not attain them, nor will you escape by fleeing.
11.11: He works, tires himself out and hurries, and only becomes poorer for it.
11.12: Such a person is without energy, calling for help, poor in strength and rich in needs, but the eyes of the Lord look favorably upon them; He lifts them from their humble condition.,
11.13: He raises his head and many are filled with admiration for him.
11.14: Good and evil, death and life, poverty and wealth come from the Lord.
11.15: God's gifts remain with the righteous, and his favor ensures prosperity forever.
11.16: Such a person becomes rich through care and frugality, and this is the share that falls to them as a reward:
11.17: He may say, "I have found rest and now I will eat from my possessions." But he does not know how much time will pass, he will leave his possessions to others and die.
11.18: Be faithful to your covenant with God and live in that thought and grow old in your work.
11.19: Do not be surprised at the troubles of the sinner; trust in the Lord and persevere in your work. For it is easy in the sight of the Lord to make the poor rich quickly and at once.
11.20: The Lord's blessing is the reward of the pious man; in a swift hour, he makes His blessing bloom.
11.21: Don't say, "What do I need, and what can my happiness be now?"«
11.22: Don't say either, "I have what I need, and what harm could I possibly have in suffering?"«
11.23: On days of happiness, one forgets misfortune, and on days of misfortune, one no longer remembers happiness.
11.24: For it is easy before the Lord, on the day of death, to repay a man according to his ways.
11.25: A moment of affliction makes one forget past well-being, and, at the end of man, his works will be revealed.
11.26: Do not consider anyone happy until their death: it is by their children that one recognizes a man.
11.27: Do not bring everyone into your house, for the snares of the deceitful man are many.
11.28: Like a hunting partridge in its cage, so is the heart of the proud, and like a spy, it lies in wait for ruin.
11.29: By turning good into evil, he sets traps and stains what is purest.
11.30: A spark produces a lot of coal, so the sinner insidiously seeks to shed blood.
11.31: Beware of the wicked, for he plots evil, lest he brand you with an indelible mark.
11.32: Give a foreigner entry into your house and he will overthrow you by stirring up trouble and alienate the people of your household.

12.1: If you do good, know to whom you are doing it and you will be thanked for your good deeds.
12.2: Do good to the pious man and you will find the reward, if not from him, then at least from the Lord.
12.3: Blessings are not for those who persist in evil, nor for those who do not practice charity.
12.4: Give to the righteous man and do not assist the sinner.
12.5: Do good to the afflicted and give not to the wicked; refuse him bread and do not give him any, lest he become stronger than you, for you will reap double evil for all the good that you have done him.
12.6: The Most High also hates sinners and will take vengeance on the wicked.
12.7: Give to the righteous man and do not aid the sinner.
12.8: It is not in prosperity that a friend attracts punishment, nor in adversity that an enemy hides.
12.9: When a man is happy, his enemies mourn; when he is unhappy, even his friend parts from him.
12.10: Never trust your enemy, for his malice is like bronze covered with rust.,
12.11: Even though he appears humble and walks bent over, watch over yourself and guard yourself from him, and you will be to him like one who polishes a mirror, and you will know that it has no rust until the end.
12.12: Do not place him beside you, lest he overthrow you and take your place. Do not seat him at your right hand, lest he seek to occupy your seat and in the end, recognizing the truth of my words, you may grieve at the remembrance of my sayings.
12.13: Who will have pity on the enchanter bitten by a snake and on all those who approach ferocious beasts?
12.14: The same applies to someone who forms a partnership with a sinner and mixes with his sins.
12.15: He will stay with you for an hour, but if you turn away, he will not last any longer.
12.16: The enemy has sweet words on his lips, but in his heart he plots how to throw you into the pit. The enemy has tears in his eyes, and if he finds the opportunity, he will be insatiable for your blood.
12.17: If misfortune befalls you, you will find it there before you and, under the pretext of helping you, it will trip you up.
12.18: Then he will nod his head, he will clap his hands, he will keep whispering and will take on a different face.

13.1: Whoever touches pitch becomes defiled, and whoever associates with the proud becomes like him.
13.2: Do not place a heavy burden on your shoulders, and do not associate with someone stronger and richer than yourself. What good can a clay pot have with a cauldron? The cauldron will strike the pot, and it will be broken.
13.3: The rich man commits an injustice and he trembles with indignation, the poor man is mistreated and he asks for forgiveness.
13.4: As long as you can be useful to him, he will use you, and when you have nothing left, he will abandon you.
13.5: If you have wealth, he will live with you, he will plunder you and have no worries.
13.6: Do you need him? He will charm you, smile at you and give you hope, he will speak beautiful words to you and say: "What do you need?"«
13.7: He will confuse you with his feasts, until he has stripped you two or three times, and in the end he will laugh at you, after which he will see you and abandon you and shake his head before you.
13.8: Beware of being seduced and, in the midst of prosperity, of falling into humiliation.
13.9: If a powerful person calls you, withdraw, he will only invite you with more instances.
13.10: Don't bump into him unexpectedly, for fear of being rejected, and don't stay too far from him, for fear of being forgotten.
13.11: Don't imagine you can talk to him as an equal and don't trust his many speeches.
13.12: For with his torrent of words he will tempt you and question you as if smiling. A merciless man, he will not keep your words to himself, nor will he spare you blows or chains.
13.13: Be careful and pay close attention, for you are walking towards your ruin.
13.14: Every living creature loves its fellow creature, and every man his neighbor.
13.15: All flesh unites according to its kind, and all man associates with his fellow man.
13.16: What union can there be between the wolf and the lamb? The same is true between the sinner and the pious man.
13.17: What peace can a hyena have with a dog? What peace can the rich have with the poor?
13.18: Onagers are prey for lions in the desert, so the poor are prey for the rich.
13.19: The proud man abhors humiliation: thus the rich man abhors the poor man.
13.20: When the rich man falters, his friends support him, but when the humble man falls, he is still rejected by his friends.
13.21: When the rich man falls, many come to his aid, he speaks foolishly and is approved. When the humble man falls, he still receives reproaches.
13.22: The rich man speaks and everyone is silent, and his speech is praised to the skies. The poor man speaks and people say, "Who is that?" and if he offends, he is knocked down.
13.23: Wealth is good when it is not united with sin, and poverty is bad in the mouth of the wicked.
13.24: A man's heart changes its face, either for good or for bad.
13.25: The sign of a contented heart is a joyful face; to find wise maxims, one needs the labor of reflection.

14.1: Blessed is the man who has not sinned with the words of his mouth, nor been bruised by the remorse of sin.
14.2: Blessed is he whose soul does not condemn him, and who has not lost hope in the Lord.
14.3: To a sordid man, wealth is useless, and what good are treasures to an envious man?
14.4: He who amasses by depriving himself amasses for others; with his possessions others will live in luxury.
14.5: He who is evil to himself, for whom will he be good? He will not enjoy his own treasures.
14.6: There is no one worse than someone who denies himself everything, and that is the just reward for his malice.
14.7: If he does any good, it is through forgetfulness, and he ends up revealing his malice.
14.8: He is a wicked man who looks with envy, who turns away his face and despises souls.
14.9: The miser's eye is not satiated by one portion, and a fatal covetousness dries up the soul.
14.10: The evil eye denies itself bread and is hungry at its own table.
14.11: My son, according to what you have, do good to yourself and present a worthy offering to the Lord.
14.12: Remember that death is near and that the pact of Sheol has not been revealed to you.
14.13: Before you die, do good to your friend and, according to your means, reach out and give to him.
14.14: Don't deprive yourself of a day of celebration and don't let any part of a good desire slip away.
14.15: Won't you leave the fruits of your labor and the fruits of your toil to the whims of fate?
14.16: Give and take and rejoice your soul, for there is no delight to be found in Sheol.
14.17: All flesh ages like a garment, for it is a law borne from the beginning, you will certainly die.
14.18: Like the green leaf on a leafy tree, it lets some fall and makes others grow, so it is with generations of flesh and blood: some die, others come to life.
14.19: Every corruptible work eventually perishes, and its maker will depart with it.
14.20: Blessed is the man who meditates on wisdom and speaks with his good sense.,
14.21: who reflects in his heart on his ways and who studies his secrets,
14.22: who pursues her, like a hunter, and watches for her entrances.
14.23: He bends down to look out of his windows and listens at his door.
14.24: He settled very close to his home and fixed his stakes in its walls
14.25: He pitches his tent beside her and lives in the house where happiness resides.
14.26: He puts his children under his protection and shelters himself under his branches.
14.27: In its shade, he will be protected from the heat and he will rest in his glory.

15.1: This is what the one who fears the Lord does, and the one who adheres to the law will obtain wisdom.
15.2: She will come to meet him like a mother and she will welcome him like a virgin bride.
15.3: She will feed him with the bread of understanding and give him the water of wisdom to drink.
15.4: He will rely on her and not waver, he will cling to her and not be put to shame.
15.5: She will raise him up before his companions and open his mouth in the midst of the assembly.
15.6: Joy, a crown of gladness, and an eternal name will be his portion.
15.7: The foolish will not possess it, and the sinners will not even see it.
15.8: She keeps away from proud men, and men of lies do not give her a second thought.
15.9: The praise of God is not pleasing in the mouth of the sinner, because it is not sent by the Lord.
15.10: For it is through wisdom that praise is dictated, and the Lord will be pleased with it.
15.11: Do not say, "The Lord is the cause of my turning away," for what he hates, you must not do.
15.12: Do not say, "He led me astray," for he has no need of the sinner.
15.13: The Lord hates all abomination, and it must not be loved by those who fear him.
15.14: In the beginning he created man and left him in the hand of his council:
15.15: «If you want to, you will keep the commandments; being faithful depends on your own pleasure.”.
15.16: He has placed fire and water before you; you may put out your hand in any direction you wish.»
15.17: Before men lie life and death; what he chooses will be given to him.
15.18: For the wisdom of the Lord is great, he is strong and mighty, and he sees all things.
15.19: His eyes are on those who fear him, and he himself knows all the works of man.
15.20: He commanded no one to be ungodly, nor gave anyone permission to sin.

16.1: Do not desire a large number of useless children, and do not find joy in ungodly sons.
16.2: If they multiply, do not rejoice, if the fear of the Lord is not with them.
16.3: Do not expect a long life for them, and do not trust in their prosperity. One is better than a thousand, and it is better to die childless than to have wicked sons.
16.4: For one intelligent man will populate a country, but the race of the wicked will be destroyed.
16.5: I have seen many examples with my own eyes and my ears have heard even more serious ones.
16.6: Fire is kindled upon the assembly of sinners, and the wrath of God is kindled upon a rebellious people.
16.7: The Lord did not forgive the ancient giants, who, confident in their strength, had rebelled.
16.8: He did not spare those who lived with Lot; he abhorred them because of their insolent pride.
16.9: He had no pity on the people destined for perdition, who were exterminated along with their sins.
16.10: Likewise, he exterminated six hundred thousand Israelites who had gathered together in the hardness of their hearts.
16.11: If only one stiffens his neck, it would be a wonder if he went unpunished. For from the Lord come mercy and wrath; mighty in pardoning, he also unleashes his anger.
16.12: As great as his mercy, so great are his punishments; he will judge man according to his deeds.
16.13: The sinner will not escape with his plunder, nor will he delay the waiting of the pious man.
16.14: He will give full rein to all his mercy, and each will receive according to their deeds.
16.15: Do not say, «I will hide from the sight of the Lord, and from above, who will think of me? In the midst of the crowd, I will be forgotten, and what am I in the midst of immense creation?»
16.16: See: the heavens and the highest heavens of God, the abyss and the earth, are shaken when he visits them,
16.17: Together the mountains and the foundations of the earth tremble when he looks at them.
16.18: But the heart of man does not reflect on this, and who is the one who studies his ways?
16.19: The storm escapes the eye of man, and most of God's works are hidden.
16.20: «"Who can tell us the works of his righteousness, and who will wait for them? The covenant of punishment is so far away."»
16.21: Thus thinks the unintelligent man, the fool who goes astray imagines only follies.
16.22: Listen to me, my son, and learn wisdom, and pay attention to my words.
16.23: I will reveal to you a doctrine weighed in the balance, and I will make known to you an exact science.
16.24: The works of the Lord have remained from the beginning as he arranged them, and from their creation he separated their parts.
16.25: He has adorned his works forever, and the most beautiful ones, so that they may shine from age to age. They feel neither hunger nor fatigue, and they do not interrupt their task.
16.26: None of them harms their neighbor and they forever obey the divine commandment.
16.27: After that, the Lord looked down upon the earth and filled it with his bounty.
16.28: He has covered the surface with animals of all kinds, and it is into his bosom that they must return.

17.1: The Lord formed man from the earth and he is returning him to the earth.
17.2: He assigned to him a specific number of days and a set time, and he gave him authority over everything on earth.
17.3: According to his nature, he clothed it by force and made it in his own image.
17.4: He instilled fear in all flesh and gave it dominion over beasts and birds.
17.5: He gave him discernment, a tongue, eyes, ears, and a heart to think.
17.6: He filled him with knowledge and understanding, and taught him good and evil.
17.7: He put his eye into their hearts, to show them the greatness of his works.
17.8: And so they will praise his holy name, by proclaiming the wonders of his works.
17.9: He also gave them knowledge and placed in their possession the law of life.
17.10: He made an everlasting covenant with them and revealed his commandments to them.
17.11: Their eyes beheld the splendors of his majesty and their ears heard the magnificent accents of his voice.
17.12: And he said to them, «Beware of all iniquity.» And he gave to each of them commandments concerning their neighbor.
17.13: Their paths are constantly before him; nothing can hide them from his sight.
17.14: To each people he assigns a leader, but Israel is the Lord's portion.
17.15: All their works are before him, like the sun, and his eyes are ever fixed upon their ways.
17.16: Their injustices are not hidden from him, and all their sins are before the Lord.
17.17: A man's charitable work is like a seal to him, and he treasures a man's good deed as the apple of his eye.
17.18: Then he will rise up and repay them according to their deeds, and he will bring down upon their own heads what is due to them.
17.19: However, to those who repent, he grants restoration and he comforts those who have lost faith.
17.20: Turn to the Lord and turn away from your sins, pray before him and diminish your offenses.
17.21: Return to the Most High, turn away from injustice and strongly hate what is abominable.
17.22: Who will praise the Most High in Sheol, in place of the living and those who pay homage to Him?
17.23: Praise is impossible for a dead man, as if he were nothing; he who has life and health praises the Lord.
17.24: How great is the mercy of the Lord and his compassion towards those who turn to him.
17.25: For not everything can be found in men, since the son of man is not immortal.
17.26: What is brighter than the sun? And yet it grows dark: so the wicked think of flesh and blood.
17.27: The sun visits the army of stars in the heights of the heavens, but all men are earth and ashes.

18.1: He who lives eternally created everything without exception.,
18.3: He gave no one the opportunity to recount his works, and who will be able to discover his greatness?
18.4: Who will express the omnipotence of His Majesty, and who will recount His mercies?
18.5: Nothing to diminish, nothing to add, impossible to penetrate the wonders of the Lord.
18.6: When a man has finished searching, he is only at the beginning, and when he stops, he does not know what to think.
18.7: What is man and what is he good for? What is his happiness and what is his unhappiness?
18.8: The number of days a man lives is at most one hundred years.
18.9: Like a drop of water taken from the sea and like a grain of sand, so were his few years on the day of eternity.
18.10: That is why the Lord is patient with mankind and shows them mercy.
18.11: He sees and acknowledges that their end is deplorable, which is why he is generous in his forgiveness.
18.12: Man's mercy is shown to his neighbor, but God's mercy extends to all flesh. He rebukes, corrects, instructs, and brings back to the fold, like a shepherd his flock.
18.13: He has pity on those who receive correction and on those who hasten to fulfill his precepts.
18.14: My son, do not add insult to your good deeds, nor add to all your gifts words that arouse sadness.
18.15: Does not the dew cool the heat of the wind? Likewise, a word is worth more than a gift.
18.16: Do you not see that speech is better than giving? The gracious man unites the two together.
18.17: The fool makes bitter reproaches, and the gift of the envious dries up the eyes.
18.18: Before you speak, educate yourself; before illness, take care of yourself.
18.19: Before the judgment, examine yourself and, at the time of the inquiry, you will find mercy.
18.20: Before you get sick, humble yourself, and when you are in sin, return to God.
18.21: Let nothing prevent you from fulfilling your vow in due time and do not wait until death to fulfill it.
18.22: Before making a vow, prepare yourself and do not be like a man who puts the Lord to the test.
18.23: Think of the wrath of the last day, the time of vengeance, when God will turn his face away.
18.24: In times of plenty, think of times of hunger; in times of wealth, think of poverty and scarcity.
18.25: From morning to evening the weather changes: so all change is swift before the Lord.
18.26: The wise man is on his guard in all things; in days of sin, he guards himself from wrongdoing.
18.27: Every sensible man knows wisdom, and pays homage to the one who has found it.
18.28: Those who grasp the words of the wise are themselves wise, and they make perfect maxims rain down.
18.29: Do not give in to your lusts and guard yourself against your desires.
18.30: If you give your soul the satisfaction of its desires, it will make you the laughingstock of your enemies.
18.31: Do not find your joy in the abundance of good food and do not be alienated by it.
18.32: Don't impoverish yourself by giving banquets with borrowed money when you have nothing in your purse.

19.1: The worker who is addicted to wine will not become rich, he who does not take care of the little he has will soon fall into ruin.
19.2: Wine and women lead intelligent men astray, and he who attaches himself to courtesans is imprudent.
19.3: The larvae and worms will make it their prey, and the criminal soul will be cut out.
19.4: He who believes too quickly is light-hearted, and he who falls into this error sins against his soul.
19.5: He who takes pleasure in foolish talk will be condemned.,
19.6: And he who hates gossip protects himself from harm.
19.7: Never repeat a word, and you will incur no harm.
19.8: Do not repeat it to a friend or an enemy, and unless you are at fault, do not reveal it.
19.9: For if he hears you, he will guard himself against you, and when the time comes, he will reveal himself as your enemy.
19.10: Have you heard anything bad? May he die with you. Don't worry, he won't kill you.
19.11: For a word to be kept, the fool is in pain, like a woman in childbirth.
19.12: Like an arrow piercing the flesh of the thigh, so is a word piercing the heart of a fool.
19.13: Ask your friend, perhaps he didn't do it, and if he did, ask him not to do it again.
19.14: Ask your friend, maybe he didn't say that, and if he did, ask him so he doesn't do it again.
19.15: Question your friend, because there is often slander and don't believe everything you hear.
19.16: There are those who are lacking, but without the heart being in it, and who has not sinned with their tongue?
19.17: Question your friend before resorting to threats and strive to observe the law of the Most High.
19.18: All wisdom consists in the fear of the Lord, and in all wisdom is the fulfillment of the law.
19.19: Wisdom is not the ability to do evil, and prudence is not found in the counsel of sinners.
19.20: There is a skill that is execrable and there is a folly that is merely a lack of wisdom.
19.21: Better is the one who has little understanding and fears God than the one who has much sense and transgresses the law.
19.22: There is a real skill involved, but one that violates justice, and such a person distorts the case to obtain the sentence he desires.
19.23: He is like a wicked man who walks bent over with grief, and his heart is full of deceit.
19.24: He lowers his head, he is deaf on one side and, as soon as he is not noticed, he takes the lead over you.
19.25: And if, through weakness, he is prevented from sinning, he will do evil when he finds the opportunity.
19.26: A man is known by his appearance, and a wise man by the face he presents.
19.27: A man's clothing, the laughter on his lips, and the way he walks reveal what he is.

20.1: There is a reprimand that is not opportune, and silence is what shows prudence.
20.2: It is better to start over than to burn with suppressed anger, and the one who confesses will be spared harm.
20.3: Like the eunuch who desires to deflower a young girl, so is he who administers justice with violence. [How beautiful it is, when one is reproved, to show repentance. In this way you will escape willful sin.]
20.4: One person shows wisdom by remaining silent, while another makes himself odious through his intemperate speech.
20.5: One person remains silent because they have nothing to say, another remains silent because they know the opportune time.
20.6: The wise man remains silent until the opportune moment, but the braggart and the reckless man overstep their bounds.
20.7: He who speaks too much will be hated, and he who gives himself free rein will make himself odious.
20.8: Such a man finds something happy in misfortune, and unexpected happiness turns to his downfall.
20.9: There is a gift that brings you nothing, and there is a gift that is returned double.
20.10: From a brilliant situation often comes damage, and one raises one's head after a humiliation.
20.11: One person buys many things at a low price, and pays seven times their value.
20.12: He who is wise in his speech is loved, but the kind words of a fool are in vain.
20.13: The gift of a fool will not profit you, for his eyes are many instead of one.
20.14: He gives little and criticizes much, and he opens his mouth like a town crier. He lends today and will ask again tomorrow: such a man is odious.
20.15: The fool says, "I have no friends, and my good deeds are not appreciated; those who eat my bread have perverse tongues."«
20.16: How many times and by how many people will he not be ridiculed?
20.17: Better a fall on the pavement than a fall of the tongue; that is how the ruin of the wicked comes swiftly.
20.18: An unpleasant man is like an out-of-season tale; the ill-mannered man constantly has it on his lips.
20.19: We do not accept a maxim from the lips of a fool, because he does not say it at the appropriate time.
20.20: He is like one who cannot sin, because of his poverty, and in his rest, he has no remorse.
20.21: Such a person is lost through false shame and falls into ruin because of the gaze of a fool.
20.22: Out of false shame, he promises his friend a great deal and unnecessarily makes an enemy of him.
20.23: Lying in man is a shameful stain; it is always on the lips of ill-mannered people.
20.24: Better a thief than a man who makes a living from lying: both will share ruin.
20.25: The habit of a liar is dishonorable, and the shame of a liar is constantly with him.
20.26: He who is wise in his speech rises in esteem, and the prudent man pleases the great.
20.27: He who cultivates his land will raise his pile of wheat, and he who pleases the powerful will have his injustices forgiven.
20.28: Gifts and presents blind the eyes of the wise and, like a muzzle on an animal's mouth, they stop blame.
20.29: Hidden wisdom, invisible treasure: what purpose do they serve?
20.30: Better is the man who hides his foolishness than the one who hides his wisdom.

21.1: My son, have you sinned? Do not do it again, but pray for your past sins.
21.2: Flee from sin as from a serpent, for if you approach it, it will bite you. Its teeth are the teeth of a lion; they bring death to men.
21.3: Every transgression is like a double-edged sword; the wound it inflicts is incurable.
21.4: Threats and insults destroy wealth; this is how the house of the proud is destroyed.
21.5: The prayer of the poor rises from his lips to the ear of the rich, but judgment will soon come upon the proud.
21.6: He who hates rebuke follows in the footsteps of the sinner, but he who fears God turns to him with a sincere heart.
21.7: A man powerful through language makes himself known from afar, but a man of sense knows when he errs.
21.8: The man who builds his house with money that is not his own is like the one who gathers his stones for winter.
21.9: The enemy troop is a mass of tow; it will eventually fall prey to the fire.
21.10: The way of the sinners is paved with stones, but at its end is the abyss of Sheol.
21.11: He who observes the law controls his thoughts, and the final result of the fear of the Lord is wisdom.
21.12: He who lacks skill will not be taught, but there is a skill that produces much bitterness.
21.13: The wisdom of the wise abounds like overflowing water, and their counsel is a fountain of life.
21.14: The inside of a fool is like a cracked vase; he will retain no knowledge.
21.15: When an intelligent man hears a wise word, he praises it and adds something to it. When a pleasure-seeker hears it, he dislikes it and throws it behind him.
21.16: The speech of a fool is like a burden on a journey, but on the lips of an intelligent man is grace.
21.17: In the assembly, we seek the mouth of the prudent man and we meditate on his words in our hearts.
21.18: Wisdom is to a fool like a ruined house, and the knowledge of a fool is but incoherent words.
21.19: Education is to fools like chains on their feet and handcuffs on their right hand.
21.20: The fool, when he laughs, makes his voice roar, but the clever man barely smiles.
21.21: Education is to a wise man like a golden ornament, and like a bracelet on the right arm.
21.22: The fool enters the house with a quick step, but the experienced man stops timidly at the entrance.
21.23: The fool stoops down at the door to see into the house, but the well-bred man stands outside.
21.24: It is rude for a man to listen at the door; a sensible man is outraged by such a shameful act.
21.25: The lips of fools utter only foolishness, but the words of prudent men are weighed in the balance.
21.26: In the mouths of fools is their heart, but the heart of the wise is their mouth.
21.27: When the wicked man curses his adversary, he curses himself.
21.28: The reporter defiles himself and is hated by all who approach him.

22.1: The lazy man is like a stone filled with garbage, and everyone hisses at his infamy.
22.2: The sloth resembles a ball of dung: the one who picks it up shakes his hand.
22.3: A badly brought-up son is a disgrace to the father who gave him life, a similarly brought-up daughter is born to his detriment.
22.4: A prudent girl will find her husband, but one who is shamed brings sorrow to her father.
22.5: The brazen girl shames her father and her husband; they will both despise her.
22.6: Like music in mourning, like a sermon out of step, but the whip and correction are at all times wisdom.
22.7: To instruct a fool is to glue a broken pot back together, [to tell something to someone who is not listening] is to wake a man from a deep sleep.
22.8: It's like talking to a sleeping man to entertain a fool; at the end of your speech, he will say, "What is it?"«
22.9: Weep for the dead, for they are deprived of light; weep for a fool, for they are deprived of wisdom. Weep softly for the dead, for they have found rest, but the life of a fool is worse than death.
22.10: Mourning for the dead lasts seven days, but for the fool and the wicked, it lasts all the days of their lives.
22.11: Do not engage in lengthy conversations with a fool, and do not associate with a senseless person. Keep away from him if you wish to avoid trouble and the defilement of his presence. Turn away from him, and you will find peace and will not be saddened by his folly.
22.12: What is heavier than lead, and what other name can be given to it than that of a fool?
22.13: Sand, salt, a mass of iron are easier to carry than an unintelligent man.
22.14: A well-connected timber frame assembly for a building will not be disjointed by an earthquake: thus the core fixed in a carefully considered design will be without fear at the critical moment.
22.15: A heart that rests on a thought of wisdom is like plaster mixed with sand on a polished wall.
22.16: A fence on a height cannot withstand the wind: so too a timid heart with its foolish resolutions will not resist fear.
22.17: He who bruises an eye makes tears flow, he who bruises a heart arouses the feeling of pain.
22.18: He who throws a stone at birds puts them to flight, and he who reproaches his friend for a kindness dissolves the friendship.
22.19: Have you drawn your sword against your friend? Don't despair, a comeback is possible.
22.20: Have you spoken ill of your friend? Have no fear, reconciliation is possible. But reproach for a kindness, arrogance, the revelation of a secret, a malicious gossip: these drive away all friends.
22.21: Remain faithful to your neighbor in his poverty, so that you may share in his prosperity. Stay with him in his days of trial, so that you may share in the good things that come to him.
22.22: Before the fire rises the steam of the furnace and the smoke, likewise before the shedding of blood resound the insults.
22.23: I will not be ashamed to defend my friend, and I will not hide from him.,
22.24: And if any harm comes to me because of him, whoever hears about it will be on their guard against him.
22.25: Who will set a guard over my mouth and a seal of prudence over my lips, lest I stumble on account of them and my tongue destroy me?.

23.1: Lord, Father and sovereign Master of my life, do not abandon me to the counsel of my lips and do not allow me to find in it an occasion of stumbling.
23.2: Who will make my thoughts feel the whip and my heart disciplined with wisdom, so as not to spare me in my follies and not to give free rein to my sins?
23.3: lest my follies increase, my sins multiply, I fall before my adversaries, and my enemy rejoice over me?
23.4: Lord, Father and God of my life, do not give me license of the eyes,
23.5: and turn away from me evil desires.
23.6: May the desires of the flesh and lust not seize me, and may you not deliver me to a shameless soul.
23.7: My children, listen to the discipline given by the mouth; whoever observes it will not be caught.
23.8: Through his lips the sinner will be caught, and the slanderer and the insolent will find an occasion for their downfall.
23.9: Do not accustom your mouth to making oaths, and do not make a habit of pronouncing the name of the Holy One.
23.10: For, just as a slave who is often tortured cannot be free from bruises, so he who takes an oath and constantly pronounces the name of the Holy One will not be pure from sin.
23.11: A man who makes many oaths multiplies his iniquity, and the whip will not depart from his house. If he has committed a sin, his guilt is upon him; if he is unaware of it, his sin is twofold. If he has sworn falsely, he will not be acquitted, for his house will be filled with punishment.
23.12: There are words that call for death: may they not be found in the inheritance of Jacob. All this is far removed from the pious; they do not engage in such sins.
23.13: Do not accustom your mouth to vile vulgarity, for it inspires guilty words.
23.14: Remember your father and mother when you sit among the great, lest you forget them in their presence and do foolish things through habit, and wish you had not been born and curse the day you were born.
23.15: A man who becomes accustomed to coarse language will never attain wisdom.
23.16: Two kinds of people multiply sins, and the third invites wrath. The person consumed by passion, like a blazing fire, will not be quenched until they are consumed. The person who is shameless in their own flesh will not cease until the fire is kindled.
23.17: To the voluptuary all bread is sweet, he will not stop until he is dead.
23.18: The man who leaves the marital bed says in his heart: "Who sees me? Darkness surrounds me, walls hide me, and no one sees me: what should I fear? The Most High will not remember my sins.".
23.19: He fears the eyes of men and does not know that the eyes of the Lord are a thousand times brighter than the sun, that they look at all the ways of man and penetrate even into hidden places.
23.20: Before they are created, all things are known to the Lord, and they are still known after they are completed.
23.21: Adultery will be punished in the streets of the city and, where he least expects it, he will be caught.
23.22: The same applies to a woman who has abandoned her husband and given an heir from a foreign union.
23.23: For first she disobeyed the law of the Most High; secondly, she sinned against her husband; and thirdly, she committed adultery and bore children of foreign blood.
23.24: She will be brought before the assembly and punishment will visit her children.
23.25: His children will not put down roots, and his branches will not bear fruit.
23.26: She will leave behind a memory doomed to damnation, and her infamy will never be erased.
23.27: And the survivors will know that there is nothing better than the fear of the Lord, nothing sweeter than keeping his commandments. [It is a great glory to follow the Lord; to cling to him is the length of one's days.].

24.1: Wisdom praises herself and glorifies herself in the midst of her people.
24.2: She opens her mouth in the assembly of the Most High and glorifies herself in the presence of His Majesty:
24.3: I came forth from the mouth of the Most High and, like a cloud, I covered the earth.
24.4: I dwelt on high, and my throne was upon a pillar of cloud.
24.5: Alone, I traversed the circle of the sky and walked in the depths of the abyss.
24.6: In the waves of the sea and over all the earth, in every people and nation I have exercised dominion.
24.7: Among them all I looked for a place to rest and in which area I should live.
24.8: Then the Creator of all things gave me his commands, and he who created me made my tent rest, and he said to me, «Dwell in Jacob, have your inheritance in Israel.»
24.9: Before all ages, from the beginning he created me and until eternity I will never cease to be.
24.10: I ministered before him in the holy tabernacle, and thus I had a permanent dwelling in Zion.
24.11: Likewise, he has made me rest in the beloved city, and in Jerusalem is the seat of my empire.
24.12: I have put down my roots in the glorified people, in the portion of the Lord, in his inheritance.
24.13: I grew up like a cedar on Lebanon and like a cypress on Mount Hermon.
24.14: I grew up like a palm tree on the shores and like roses in Jericho, like a beautiful olive tree in the plain, and I grew up like a plane tree.
24.15: I gave perfume like cinnamon and like fragrant balm and like choice myrrh I spread a sweet fragrance, like galbanum, onyx and stacte and like the vapor of incense in the tabernacle.
24.16: I have spread out my branches like the terebinth tree, and my boughs are branches of glory and grace.
24.17: Like the vine, I have produced lovely shoots, and my flowers have borne fruits of glory and riches. [I am the mother of pure love, fear of the Lord, knowledge, and holy hope].
24.18: Come to me, all you who desire me, and be filled with my fruits.
24.19: For my memory is sweeter than honey, and my possession sweeter than the honeycomb.
24.20: Those who eat me will be hungry again, and those who drink me will be thirsty again.
24.21: Whoever listens to me will never be confused, and those who act through me will not sin.
24.22: All this is the book of the covenant of the Most High God, this is the law that Moses gave, to be the inheritance of the assemblies of Jacob.
24.23: This law causes Wisdom to overflow, like the Phison and the Tigris in the time of new fruits.
24.24: It pours forth wisdom in a flood, like the Euphrates, like the Jordan at harvest time.
24.25: It brings forth science, like the River, like the Gehon at harvest time.
24.26: The first person who studied it did not finish knowing it, and likewise, the last person did not penetrate it.
24.27: For his intelligence is vaster than the sea and his counsel deeper than the great abyss.
24.28: And I, I flowed like a canal diverted from a river, like an aqueduct watering a paradise.
24.29: I said, "I will water my garden, I will water my flowerbed." And behold, my canal became a river, my river became a sea.
24.30: Therefore, I want to make education shine brighter than the dawn, and to spread its maxims far and wide,
24.31: I still want to spread the doctrine like a prophecy and leave it as a legacy to distant generations.
24.32: Acknowledge that I have not worked for myself alone, but for all those who seek Wisdom.

25.1: Three things please me and are beautiful in the sight of the Lord and men: harmony among brothers, friendship among relatives, and good accord between husband and wife.
25.2: But there are three kinds of people I hate and whose lives I find utterly odious: the proud poor man, the rich man who uses fraud, and the voluptuous old man, devoid of sense.
25.3: You did not accumulate wealth in your youth: how will you possess wealth in your old age?
25.4: How wonderful it is for white hair to judge well, for old age to know good advice.
25.5: May wisdom befit the elderly, and prudence and counsel those who are honored.
25.6: The crown of the elderly is a rich experience; their glory is the fear of the Lord.
25.7: There are nine things that in my heart I consider blessed, and a tenth that my tongue proclaims: A man who rejoices in his children, and lives long enough to see the ruin of his enemies.
25.8: Blessed is he who dwells with a wise wife and he who does not sin with his tongue. [Blessed is he who has found a faithful friend] and he who does not serve unworthy masters.
25.9: Blessed is he who has found prudence, and blessed is he who teaches it to a listening ear.
25.10: How great is the man who has found wisdom! Yet he is not above him who fears the Lord.
25.11: The fear of the Lord surpasses all things; whoever possesses it, to whom can he be compared? [The fear of the Lord is the beginning of his love, and faith is the beginning of devotion to God].
25.12: All suffering, but not the suffering of the heart; all wickedness, but not the wickedness of a woman.
25.13: All evils, but not the evil caused by adversaries; all vengeances, but not the vengeance of enemies.
25.14: There is no venom worse than snake venom and there is no anger greater than a woman's anger.
25.15: I would rather live with a lion and a dragon than remain with a wicked woman.
25.16: The woman's wickedness changes her appearance, she makes her face as black as a sack.
25.17: Her husband goes to sit in the middle of his friends and, hearing them, he sighs bitterly.
25.18: All wickedness is light, compared to the wickedness of a woman: may the fate of sinners fall upon her.
25.19: Like a sandy slope for an old man's feet, so is a talkative wife for a quiet husband.
25.20: Do not be seduced by the beauty of a woman, and let no woman arouse your lust.
25.21: It is a cause for indignation, disgrace, and great shame that a woman should provide for her husband.
25.22: A broken heart, a sad face, a suffering soul: these are the fruits of a wicked woman. Withered hands and buckling knees: these are the fruits of a woman who does not make her husband happy.
25.23: It was through a woman that sin began, and it is because of her that we all die.
25.24: Leave no escape for the water, nor any authority for the wicked woman.
25.25: If it does not walk as your hand directs it, cut it off from your flesh.

26.1: Happy is the husband of a virtuous woman, for the number of his days will be doubled.
26.2: A strong wife is the joy of her husband and he spends his years in peace.
26.3: The virtuous woman is a good portion, she will be given as an inheritance to those who fear the Lord.
26.4: Rich or poor, her husband has a joyful heart, his face is always cheerful.
26.5: There are three things my heart dreads, and concerning the fourth, I pray before the Lord: the evil talk of a whole city, the cursing of the crowd, and slander: all these things are more hateful to me than death.,
26.6: But the pain of the heart and the affliction, it is a woman jealous of another and the whip of a tongue that tells everyone its grievances.
26.7: A wicked wife is like a pair of oxen in disagreement; the one who holds her is like the one who has caught a scorpion.
26.8: It is a great cause for anger that a woman is given to wine; she will not even conceal her shame.
26.9: The brazenness of her gaze, the blinking of her eyelids, reveals the immodesty of a woman.
26.10: Keep a close watch over a wayward girl, lest, seeing your negligence, she should give herself over to debauchery.
26.11: Beware of following an impudent eye, otherwise do not be surprised if it leads you into sin.
26.12: As the thirsty traveler opens his mouth and drinks of whatever water he finds, the shameless man sits before every post and opens his quiver before the arrow.
26.13: A wife's grace brings joy to her husband, and her intelligence adds strength to his bones.
26.14: A silent woman is a gift from God, and nothing compares to a well-bred woman.
26.15: A modest woman is a grace above all graces, and no treasure is worth a chaste woman.
26.16: The sun rises in the heights of the Lord: so the beauty of a woman shines in her well-adorned house.
26.17: Like the torch that shines on the sacred candlestick, so is the beauty of the face on a noble stature.
26.18: Like golden columns on silver bases, such are elegant feet on solid heels.
26.19: Two things grieve my heart and the third arouses my indignation: the warrior who suffers from poverty, the intelligent who are despised, the one who passes from righteousness to sin, the Lord prepares him for the sword.
26.20: The merchant will hardly avoid making a mistake, and the wine merchant will not be exempt from sin.

27.1: Many sin for money, and those who seek to get rich look away.
27.2: The peg is driven between the joints of the stones: thus sin enters between the sale and the purchase.
27.3: If you do not hold fast to the fear of God, your house will soon be destroyed.
27.4: When you shake the sieve, a pile of refuse remains: similarly, a man's faults appear in his speeches.
27.5: The furnace tests the potter's vessels, and the man's test is in his conversation.
27.6: The fruit of a tree reveals the field that bears it: so too does speech reveal the feelings of the human heart.
27.7: Do not praise anyone before they have spoken, for speech is the test of men.
27.8: If you pursue justice, you will find it and you will be clothed in it as in a robe of honor.
27.9: Birds gather with their own kind: likewise, truth returns to those who practice it.
27.10: The lion lies in wait for its prey: so sin lies in wait for those who commit injustice.
27.11: The speech of a pious man is always wisdom, but the fool is changeable like the moon.
27.12: To go among fools, observe the time, but always be among those who reflect.
27.13: The conversation of fools is detestable; their laughter bursts forth in the joy of sin.
27.14: The language of the one who utters oaths makes hair stand on end; when he argues, one covers one's ears.
27.15: The disputes of the proud cause bloodshed and their insults are painful to hear.
27.16: He who reveals secrets loses trust and will no longer find a friend to his liking.
27.17: Love your friend and be loyal to him, but if you reveal his secrets, don't chase after him.
27.18: For, as a man kills his enemy, so you have killed the affection of your friend.
27.19: And just as when you let a bird escape from your hand, so you have driven your friend away and you will never catch him again.
27.20: Do not pursue him, for he is far away; he has fled like a gazelle escaped from the net.
27.21: We bandage a wound, we reconcile after an insult, but he who has revealed secrets has no hope left.
27.22: He who winks his eye creates iniquity, and no one can get rid of it.
27.23: In your presence, he will only have sweetness on his lips, he will admire your words, but afterwards he will change his language and give an unfortunate turn to your speeches.
27.24: I hate many things, but nothing so much as him; even the Lord detests him.
27.25: He who throws a stone into the air throws it on his own head: so a treacherous blow wounds the treacherous one.
27.26: Whoever digs a pit will fall into it, and whoever sets a net will be caught in it.
27.27: He who does evil will see it roll back upon him, and he will not know where it came from.
27.28: Sarcasm and insult are in the mouths of the proud, but vengeance lies in wait for them like a lion.
27.29: Those who rejoice in the misfortune of pious men will be trapped, and their grief will consume them before their death.
27.30: Resentment and anger are also detestable, and the sinner possesses them.

28.1: He who seeks revenge will find vengeance with the Lord, who will carefully preserve his sins.
28.2: Forgive your neighbor's injustice, and then, through your prayer, your sins will be forgiven.
28.3: The man harbors anger towards another man and asks God for healing.
28.4: He has no pity for a man, his fellow man, and he begs for his own sins.
28.5: He, who is only flesh, holds a grudge; who then will obtain for him forgiveness of his sins?
28.6: Remember your end and cease to hate, corruption and death, and observe the commandments.
28.7: Remember the commandments and bear no grudge against your neighbor, the covenant of the Most High, and overlook the offense.
28.8: Stay away from arguments and you will sin less, for an angry person stirs up conflict.
28.9: and the sinner sows discord among friends and spreads slander among those who live at peace.
28.10: Fire ignites in proportion to the fuel that feeds it: so too does a man's anger ignite in proportion to his power. According to his wealth, he raises his fury; it ignites according to the violence of the dispute.
28.11: A rushed quarrel ignites a fire, and a thoughtless argument leads to bloodshed.
28.12: If you blow on a spark, it ignites; if you spit on it, it goes out: both things come out of your mouth.
28.13: Curse the gossip and the two-tongued man, for they have lost many who lived in peace.
28.14: The slanderous tongue has driven many of them out and driven them from one people to another; it has overthrown strong cities and toppled the palaces of the great.
28.15: Slanderous tongues have driven out valiant women and robbed them of the fruits of their labor.
28.16: Whoever listens to him will find no rest and will have no peace in his home.
28.17: A blow from a stick causes bruises, a blow from the tongue breaks bones.
28.18: Many perished by the edge of the sword, but not as many as perished by the tongue.
28.19: Blessed is he who is safe from it, who has not been given over to its fury, who has not borne its yoke and has not been bound by its chains.
28.20: For his yoke is an iron yoke and his chains are bronze chains.
28.21: The death it brings is a terrible death, and Sheol is better than it.
28.22: It will have no power over pious men, and they will not be consumed by its flame.
28.23: Those who abandon the Lord will fall into it, and it will consume them without being quenched.
28.24: See, surround your domain with a hedge of thorns, bind your gold and silver in your bag,
28.25: And make a scale and weights for your speeches, and a door and a lock for your mouth.
28.26: Be careful not to falter with your tongue, lest you fall before the one who lies in wait for you.

29.1: He who practices mercy lends to his neighbor, and he who supports him with his hand observes the commandments.
29.2: Lend to your neighbor when he is in need and, in turn, give back to your neighbor, when the time comes, what he has lent you.
29.3: Keep your word and act loyally with him, and you will find at all times what you need.
29.4: Many people see what they have been lent as a find and cause trouble for those who have helped them.
29.5: Until we have received, we kiss the hand of our neighbor, in a humble voice we praise his riches, but when the time comes to give back, we delay, we only give words of complaint and we blame the harshness of the times.
29.6: If one has the means, the lender will receive barely half and will think he's made a bargain. If one doesn't, one deprives him of his money, and he unwittingly makes an enemy of his debtor, who repays him with curses and insults and who, instead of honor, returns only outrage.
29.7: Many refuse to lend because of the malice of men; they fear being unnecessarily deprived of their money.
29.8: However, be lenient towards the unfortunate and do not make him wait for your alms.
29.9: Assist the poor because of the divine commandment and, because of their distress, do not send them away empty-handed.
29.10: Agree to lose your money in favor of your brother and your friend, and let it not rust without profit under a stone.
29.11: Use your treasure according to the precepts of the Most High, and it will benefit you more than gold.
29.12: Keep your alms in your rooms and they will deliver you from all misfortune.
29.13: Better than a strong shield, better than a powerful spear, it will fight for you in the face of the enemy.
29.14: A good man stands surety for his neighbor, and only he who has lost all shame abandons him.
29.15: Do not forget the kindness of the one who answered, for he made a commitment for you.
29.16: The sinner causes his protector to lose his possessions, and the ungrateful man abandons his savior.
29.17: A bond has led to the loss of many happy people and tossed them about like the waves of the sea.,
29.18: She banished powerful men, and they were forced to wander among foreign nations.
29.19: The sinner is quick to offer guarantees, and he who pursues gain will experience the severity of the judgments.
29.20: Help your neighbor according to your ability, and take care not to fall into misfortune yourself.
29.21: The first things for living are water and bread, clothing and a house to cover nakedness.
29.22: Better the life of a poor man under a wooden roof than sumptuous food in a stranger's house.
29.23: Whether you have little or much, be content, [and you will not be accused of being a stranger.]
29.24: It is a sad life to go from house to house, where you are received as a stranger, where you do not dare to open your mouth.
29.25: You will give your guest food and drink without being thanked, and you will also hear bitter words on top of that:
29.26: «"Come, stranger, prepare the table, and if you have anything, give me something to eat.".
29.27: "Go away, stranger, from this magnificence; I have my brother to receive, I need my home."»
29.28: It is hard for someone with sense to be criticized for their hospitality and insulted by their debtor.

30.1: He who loves his son often makes him feel the whip, so that he may later have joy from it.
30.2: He who raises his son well will reap benefits from him and will boast of him before his acquaintances.
30.3: He who instructs his son will make his enemy jealous, and he will rejoice over him in the presence of his friends.
30.4: If his father dies, it's as if he hasn't died, because he leaves behind someone who resembles him.
30.5: During his life, he sees it and rejoices, and at his death, he is not grieved.
30.6: He leaves behind someone who will avenge him against his enemies and show gratitude to his friends.
30.7: He who spoils his son will bandage his wounds, and at each of his cries, his insides will be moved.
30.8: The untamed horse becomes intractable: thus the son left to his own devices becomes reckless.
30.9: Caress your child and he will make you tremble, play with him and he will sadden you.
30.10: Do not laugh with him, lest you have to grieve with him and in the end you gnash your teeth.
30.11: Don't give him complete freedom in his youth and don't turn a blind eye to his follies.
