30.12: [Bend his head in his youth and bruise his sides while he is a child, lest he become stubborn and disobey you, [and you have sorrow in your heart.]
30.13: Correct your son and make him work, lest he stumble through your shameful weakness.
30.14: Better a poor person who is healthy and vigorous than a rich person whose body is scourged by disease.
30.15: Health and a good complexion are worth more than all the gold, and a vigorous body is preferable to an immense fortune.
30.16: There is no wealth preferable to the health of the body and there is no joy better than the joy of the heart.
30.17: Death is better than a life of bitterness, and eternal rest is better than continual suffering.
30.18: Wealth poured out over a closed mouth is like offerings of food placed on a grave.
30.19: What good is an offering to an idol? It will neither eat it nor smell its fragrance.
30.20: So it is with the man whom God pursues with illness: He sees with his own eyes and he sighs, like a eunuch who holds a virgin in his arms.
30.21: Do not abandon your soul to sadness and do not torment yourself with your thoughts.
30.22: Joy in the heart is the life of man, and the gladness of man is for him length of days.
30.23: Love your soul and comfort your heart and drive sadness far from you, grief has killed many and there is no profit in it.
30.24: Outbursts of anger and temper shorten our days, and worries bring on old age before our time.
30.25: A generous and kind heart takes care of the food that makes up its sustenance.

31.1: The rich man's sleepless nights consume his flesh, and his worries rob him of sleep.
31.2: Perpetual worry prevents sleep, just as a serious illness banishes it.
31.3: The rich man works to amass wealth and, when he rests, he indulges in pleasures.
31.4: The poor man works because he has nothing else to live on, and when he rests, he lacks everything.
31.5: He who loves gold will not be without sin, and he who pursues corruption will be filled.
31.6: Many were brought to ruin because of gold, and their downfall lay before them.
31.7: Gold is a source of scandal for those who sacrifice to it; every fool will be caught in it.
31.8: Blessed is the rich man who is found without blemish, and who did not go after gold.
31.9: Who is he, that we should call him blessed? For he has done a wonderful thing among his people.
31.10: Who is there who has been tested in this matter and found blameless? Let that test be to him a cause of glory. Who could have broken the law and not broken it, or done wrong and not done it?
31.11: His fortune will be strengthened and the assembly will publicize his good deeds.
31.12: If you sit down at a well-laden table, do not open your mouth before it and say, "There is much food on it."«
31.13: Remember that the envious eye is evil; has anything been created worse than the envious eye? Therefore it weeps with every face.
31.14: Where he is looking, do not extend your hand and do not bump into him in the dish.
31.15: Judge the desires of others according to yourself, and act in all things with consideration.
31.16: Eat, as is proper for a man, from what is before you, and do not chew noisily, lest you inspire disgust.
31.17: Stop first out of good manners and don't appear insatiable, for fear of causing scandal.
31.18: If you are sitting in a large group, do not extend your hand before others.
31.19: How little a well-bred man needs. And so, on his bed, he breathes freely.
31.20: Restful sleep is good for a sober stomach; one wakes up in the morning with a clear mind. The pains of insomnia, vomiting, and colic are for the intemperate man.
31.21: If you have been led to overeat, get up, walk around outside and you will feel relieved.
31.22: Listen to me, my son, and do not despise me, and in the end you will experience the truth of my words: In all your actions, be diligent, and no illness will befall you.
31.23: Lips bless the one who is generous in his meals, and the testimony given to his generosity is true.
31.24: The city murmurs against the one who is stingy with his meals, and the testimony given to his stinginess is accurate.
31.25: Don't be a braggart with wine, for wine has destroyed many.
31.26: The furnace tests steel when it is tempered, just as wine tests hearts when the proud quarrel.
31.27: Wine is like life to man, if you drink it in moderation. What life is there for someone who lacks wine? And indeed, wine was made to bring joy to mankind.
31.28: Joy of the heart and gladness of the soul, such is wine taken at the right time, in the right measure.
31.29: Bitterness of the soul is like wine drunk in abundance, while one is excited and arguing.
31.30: Drunkenness inflames the fury of the fool and makes him fall, it diminishes his strength and brings about injuries.
31.31: At a feast, do not reproach your neighbor and do not treat him with contempt while he is enjoying himself, do not address him with insulting words and do not press him, asking him for something again.

32.1: Have you been made king of the feast? Don't exalt yourself; be among the guests like one of them. Take care of them and then sit down.
32.2: When you have fulfilled all your duties, take your place, so that you may rejoice because of them and, for the beautiful order of the banquet, receive the crown.
32.3: Speak, old man, for it suits you, but with accuracy and doctrine and without hindering the music.
32.4: When listening to music, do not lavish words or display your wisdom out of sync.
32.5: A carbuncle seal set in gold, like a harmonious concert at a banquet.
32.6: An emerald seal in a gold setting, like a sweet melody with a pleasant wine.
32.7: Speak, young man, if it is useful to you, barely twice, if you are questioned.
32.8: Keep your speech concise; say a lot in a few words. Be like a man who has knowledge and knows when to be silent.
32.9: In the company of the powerful, do not consider yourself their equal, and where there are old men, be sparing in your speech.
32.10: Before the thunder, the lightning flashes: thus grace walks before the modest young man.
32.11: When the time comes, get up from the table without delay, run to your house and do not be careless.
32.12: There, have fun, do what you like, but without being boastful.
32.13: And for all this, bless the One who created you and who fills you with all his blessings.
32.14: Those who fear the Lord receive instruction, and those who earnestly seek him will find his favor.
32.15: He who seeks the law will find his satisfaction in it, but for the hypocrite, it will be an occasion of stumbling.
32.16: Those who fear the Lord will find true justice and will make righteous judgments shine forth like a torch.
32.17: The sinner declines correction and makes excuses as he pleases.
32.18: An intelligent man despises no advice, but the foreigner and the proud man are not deterred by any fear and, after acting, they are without reflection [and thus they are convicted of folly].
32.19: Do nothing without thinking, and afterwards you will have no regrets.
32.20: Don't go on a rough path and you won't bump into stones.
32.21: Don't rely on a single path and be on your guard with regard to your children.
32.22: In everything you do, trust in your soul, for this too is observance of the commandments.
32.23: He who trusts in the law is attentive to its precepts, and he who trusts in the Lord will suffer no harm.

33.1: No harm will befall the one who fears the Lord, but if he is tempted, he will be delivered.
33.2: A wise man does not hate the law, but he who is hypocritical towards it is like a ship in a storm.
33.3: The intelligent man trusts in the law and for him the law is trustworthy like the oracle of the Urim.
33.4: Prepare your speech and you will be listened to; gather your knowledge and respond.
33.5: The inside of a fool is like a chariot wheel, and his thinking like a turning axle.
33.6: The stallion is the image of the mocking friend: it neighs under every rider.
33.7: Why does one day prevail over another, since the light of every day of the year comes from the sun?
33.8: It is the wisdom of the Lord who established distinctions between them, instituting different times and festivals.
33.9: Among the days, there are some that he has exalted and sanctified, and there are some that he has placed among the ordinary days.
33.10: Thus all men come from dust, from the earth from which Adam was formed.
33.11: But with great wisdom the Lord distinguished them and led them in different ways.
33.12: He blessed some and exalted them, he sanctified others and drew them near to himself. Others he cursed and humbled and cast down from their places.
33.13: Just as clay is in the potter's hand and he disposes of it according to his good pleasure, so men are in the hand of Him who made them and He gives to them according to His judgment.
33.14: Opposite evil is good, opposite death is life: so opposite the righteous is the sinner.
33.15: Consider in the same way all the works of the Most High: they are two by two, one opposed to the other.
33.16: For me, coming last, I devoted my vigils to wisdom, like one who gleans grapes after the harvest, by the blessing of the Lord, I took the lead and, like the harvester, I filled the winepress.
33.17: Acknowledge that I have not worked for myself alone, but for all those who seek wisdom.
33.18: Listen to me, leaders of the people, presidents of the assembly, give me your ear.
33.19: Do not give power over you during your lifetime to your son or wife, nor to your brother or friend, and do not give your possessions to another, lest you, full of regret, have to address supplications to them.
33.20: As long as you live and have breath left, do not alienate yourself to any flesh.
33.21: Because it is better for your children to ask you than for you to have to look into the hands of your children.
33.22: In everything you do, remain the master, and do not tarnish your reputation.
33.23: When the end of your days comes and the time comes for you to die, distribute your inheritance.
33.24: To the donkey the fodder, the stick and the load, to the slave the bread, the punishment and the work.
33.25: Make your slave work and you will be at rest, leave his hands free and he will seek freedom.
33.26: The yoke and the strap force the neck to bend, torture and pain to the wicked slave.
33.27: Send him to work, so that he will not be idle, for idleness teaches many evils.
33.28: Put him to work, that is what suits him, and if he does not obey, tighten the shackles on him, but do not exceed the bounds towards anyone and do nothing contrary to justice.
33.29: If you have a slave, let him be like yourself, for you acquired him with blood.
33.30: If you have a slave, treat him as yourself, and in this way you will bind him to your soul.
33.31: If you mistreat him and he runs away, which way will you go to look for him?

34.1: The fool indulges in vain and deceptive hopes, and dreams give wings to fools.
34.2: To dwell on dreams is like trying to grasp a shadow and reach the wind.
34.3: One thing after another, that's what we see in dreams, it's like the image of a face facing another face.
34.4: From impurity, what pure thing can come? From falsehood, what true thing can come?
34.5: Divination, auguries, and dreams are vain things, like the imaginings of the heart of a pregnant woman.
34.6: Unless they are sent by the Most High on a visit, do not surrender your heart to them.
34.7: For dreams have led many astray, and those who placed their hopes in them have fallen.
34.8: But the law is fulfilled without falsehood, as is every wise word that comes from a faithful mouth.
34.9: The educated man knows many things, and the man of great experience speaks wisely.
34.10: He who has not been tested knows little, and he who has traveled possesses great prudence.
34.11: I have seen many things in my travels and my knowledge is greater than my words.
34.12: I have often been in mortal danger and I have been saved thanks to this experience.
34.13: The spirit of those who fear the Lord will live, for their hope is in him who saves them.
34.14: He who fears the Lord is not afraid of anything, he does not tremble, for God is his hope.
34.15: Blessed is the soul of the one who fears the Lord. On whom does it rely, and who is its support?
34.16: The eyes of the Lord are on those who love him; he is a mighty protector, a strong support, a shelter from the east wind, a shelter from the southern heat, a guard against stumbling, a help against falling.,
34.17: It uplifts the soul, it illuminates the eyes, it gives health, life and blessing.
34.18: To sacrifice ill-gotten gains is to make a paltry offering, and the mockery of sinners cannot be pleasing to God.
34.19: The Most High does not accept the offerings of the wicked, and it is not for the sake of many victims that he forgives sins.
34.20: He sacrifices a son before the eyes of his father, the one who offers a sacrifice taken from the property of the poor.
34.21: The bread of the unfortunate is the life of the poor; he who deprives them of it is a murderer.
34.22: He who deprives his neighbor of his sustenance gives death; he who deprives the mercenary of his wages sheds blood.
34.23: One builds and the other destroys: what do they gain from it, if not pain?
34.24: One prays and the other curses: whose voice will the Master listen to?
34.25: What does someone who washes themselves after contact with a dead person, and then touches them again, gain from having washed themselves?
34.26: So it is with the man who fasts for his sins, if he is going to commit them again: who will hear his prayer and what will his humiliation do him?.

35.1: Those who observe the law make many offerings,
35.2: He who adheres to the precepts offers a peaceful sacrifice.
35.3: Those who give thanks make an offering of fine flour.,
35.4: He who practices mercy offers a sacrifice of praise.
35.5: What pleases the Lord is turning away from evil; what obtains his forgiveness is fleeing from injustice.
35.6: Yet do not appear before the Lord empty-handed,
35.7: because all these offerings must be made because of the precept.
35.8: The offering of the righteous fattens the altar, and its sweet aroma rises before the Lord.
35.9: The sacrifice of the righteous man is pleasing, and his memory will not be forgotten.
35.10: Glorify the Lord with a generous heart and do not withhold anything from the firstfruits of your hands.
35.11: In all your offerings, have a joyful countenance and dedicate your tithe with gladness.
35.12: Give to the Most High according to what he has given you, with a generous heart, according to what your hands have earned.
35.13: For the Lord will repay, and he will give you back seven times as much.
35.14: Do not try to bribe him with gifts, for he will not receive them.
35.15: and do not rely on an unjust offering. For the Lord is a judge and does not show partiality.
35.16: He shows no partiality to the detriment of the poor and he listens to the prayer of the oppressed.
35.17: He does not disdain the orphan who begs, nor the widow who pours out her complaint.
35.18: Do not the widow's tears flow down her cheeks?,
35.19: And does not his cry burst forth upon the one who causes them to be poured out?
35.20: He who honors God in the way that pleases Him will be well received and his prayer will rise to the heavens.
35.21: The humble man's prayer will penetrate the clouds; he will not be consoled until it reaches God; he will not cease until the Most High has looked upon him, and the Lord will judge according to equity and render justice.
35.22: The Lord will not delay, nor will he have patience with the oppressors, until he has broken the backs of these merciless men.,
35.23: and that he should take vengeance on the nations, until he had annihilated the multitude of blasphemers and shattered the scepters of the wicked,
35.24: until he has repaid to man according to his deeds and rewarded men for their works according to their thoughts,
35.25: until he took up the cause of his people and gladdened them with his mercy.
35.26: Mercy is beautiful in the time of Israel's oppression, like rain-laden clouds in the time of drought.

36.1: Have mercy on us, Master, God of the universe, and look,
36.2: and spread your terror over all nations.
36.3: Raise your hand against foreign peoples and let them feel your power.
36.4: Just as you showed your holiness before them by punishing us, so show your greatness before us by chastising them.,
36.5: and that they may learn, as we ourselves have learned, that there is no God but you, Lord.
36.6: Renew the wonders and reproduce the marvels,
36.7: Glorify your right hand and arm.
36.8: Awaken your wrath and unleash your anger,
36.9: Destroy the opponent and annihilate the enemy.
36.10: Hasten the time and remember the oath, and let your great deeds be celebrated.
36.11: May he who escaped be consumed by a blazing fire, and may those who mistreat your people meet their destruction.
36.12: Crush the heads of the enemy leaders, who say, "There is only us."«
36.13: Gather all the tribes of Jacob and restore to them the inheritance as in the beginning.
36.14: Lord, have mercy on your people, who are called by your name, and on Israel, whom you have made like a firstborn son.
36.15: Have compassion on the city of your sanctuary, Jerusalem, the place of your rest.
36.16: Fill Zion with your prophecies and your people with your glory.
36.17: Bear witness to those who are your creatures from the beginning and fulfill the promises made in your name.
36.18: Reward those who hope in you, and may your prophets be found true. Hear, O Lord, the prayers of those who implore you.,
36.19: according to the blessing of Aaron upon your people, and that all the inhabitants of the earth acknowledge that you are the Lord, the God of ages.
36.20: The stomach receives all kinds of food, but some foods are better than others.
36.21: The palate discerns wild meat by taste: thus the sensible heart recognizes a lying word.
36.22: A perverse heart causes sorrow, but a man of experience guards against it.
36.23: A woman can marry any kind of husband, but one girl is better than another.
36.24: A woman's beauty delights the face and surpasses all of a man's desires.
36.25: If kindness and gentleness are on her tongue, her husband is no longer a mere child of men.
36.26: He who acquires a wife has the foundation of his fortune, a helper equal to himself, and a pillar of support.
36.27: Where there is no hedge, the estate is plundered; where there is no woman, the wandering man groans.
36.28: Who trusts the agile brigand, who runs from town to town? So it is with the man who has no home and who takes his shelter wherever night finds him.

37.1: Every friend says, "I too am your friend," but such a friend is only a friend in name.
37.2: Is it not a grief unto death, when a companion and a friend turn into enemies?
37.3: O perverse thought, from where did you come, to cover the earth with deceit?
37.4: A friend's companion rejoices in his joys, and in the day of adversity, he turns against him.
37.5: A companion shares his friend's sorrow for the sake of his stomach, and in the face of battle, he takes up his shield.
37.6: Do not forget your friend in your heart and, amidst opulence, do not lose his memory.
37.7: Every advisor gives advice, but some advise in their own interest.
37.8: When dealing with an advisor, be on your guard and first try to find out what his interest is, for he will advise in his own interest, so that he does not cast a spell on you.,
37.9: and let him not say, "Your way is good," then he will stand on the other side to see what will happen to you.
37.10: Do not consult a man who looks at you from below, and hide your resolve from the one who envies you.
37.11: Do not consult a woman about her rival, a coward about war, a merchant about a trade, a buyer about a sale, an envious person about gratitude, a heartless person about a charitable act, a lazy person about any work, a household employee about the completion of a job, a lazy slave about a heavy task: do not rely on these people for any advice.
37.12: But be diligent in the presence of a godly man, whom you know to be observant of the commandments, whose heart is like your own heart, and who, if you fall, will suffer with you.
37.13: Then stick to what your heart tells you, because no one is more faithful to you than it.
37.14: Because the soul of man sometimes reveals more than seven sentinels posted on a height to observe.
37.15: And with all this, pray to the Most High, so that He may surely guide your path.
37.16: Before all work comes the word; before all undertaking, reflection.
37.17: Four things appear as evidence of the change in the heart:
37.18: good and evil, life and death, and language is always their mistress.
37.19: Such a man is prudent and a teacher to many, but he is useless to himself.
37.20: He who feigns wisdom in his words is odious; he will eventually lack all sustenance.
37.21: For the Lord has not shown him favor, because he lacks all wisdom.
37.22: Such a wise person is wise for themselves, and the fruits of their knowledge are assured on their lips.
37.23: The wise man instructs his people, and the fruits of his knowledge are assured.
37.24: The wise man is filled with blessings and all who see him proclaim him happy.
37.25: Man's life is but a limited number of days, but the days of Israel are without number.
37.26: The wise man gains the trust of his people, and his name will live forever.
37.27: My son, regarding your way of life, consult your soul, see what is harmful to it and do not give it to it.
37.28: Because not everything is good for everyone, and not everyone finds well-being in everything.
37.29: Don't be insatiable over every treat and don't greedily devour food.,
37.30: because excessive food intake causes discomfort, and intemperance leads to colic.
37.31: Many have died because of intemperance, but he who abstains prolongs his life.

38.1: Give the doctor the honor that is due to him for your needs, for he too was created by the Lord.
38.2: Indeed, healing comes from the Most High, and he receives gifts from the king himself.
38.3: The doctor's knowledge raises his head and he is admired in the presence of the great.
38.4: The Lord makes the earth produce its medicines, and the wise man does not disdain them.
38.5: Has not a piece of wood softened bitter water, so that its virtue might be known to all?
38.6: He gave men knowledge, to glorify himself through his marvelous gifts.
38.7: Through them, man brings about healing and removes pain.
38.8: The pharmacist makes mixtures from it and his work is scarcely finished before well-being spreads across the face of the earth through him.
38.9: My son, if you are sick, do not neglect my advice, but pray to the Lord and he will heal you.
38.10: Turn away from transgression, straighten your hands, and purify your heart from all sin.
38.11: Offer incense and flour offerings and sacrifice fat victims, as if it were the end of you.
38.12: Then give access to the physician, for he too was created by the Lord, and do not let him leave you, for you need him.
38.14: for they too will pray to the Lord, that he may grant them rest and healing, to prolong the life of the sick.
38.15: Let he who sins before his Creator fall into the hands of the physician.
38.16: My son, shed tears over the dead and, as if you were suffering terribly, begin your lamentation. Then give his body the care it deserves and do not neglect his burial.
38.17: Shed bitter tears, exhale burning sighs, and mourn, as is fitting, for a day or two, to avoid gossip. Then console yourself, to banish sadness.,
38.18: for from sadness can come death, and grief of the heart overwhelms all strength.
38.19: When you take away a dead person, grief must pass with them, just as the life of the poor is against their heart.
38.20: Do not abandon your heart to sadness, chase it away, remembering your end.
38.21: Don't forget: there is no going back, you will not be of any use to the dead man and you will only harm yourself.
38.22: Remember that the judgment made on him will be the same for you: "For me yesterday, for you today."«
38.23: When the dead person rests, let their memory rest and find comfort in their passing spirit.
38.24: The scribe's wisdom is acquired through leisure, and he who does not have to concern himself with business will become wise.
38.25: How could he become wise who governs the plow, whose ambition is to wield the goad as a lance, who drives his oxen and meddles in their work and knows how to speak only of the young of bulls?
38.26: He puts his whole heart into tracing furrows, and takes vigilant care in providing fodder for his heifers.
38.27: The same is true of every carpenter and builder, who pursues their occupations night and day, of the one who engraves the impressions of seals: his application is to vary the figures, he puts his heart into reproducing the design, a vigilant care to perfect his work.
38.28: Such is the blacksmith, seated by his anvil, contemplating the still-raw iron; the steam of the fire melts his flesh, yet he endures the heat of the furnace, the sound of the hammer deafening his ear, his eye fixed on the model of the tool. He puts his heart into perfecting his work, vigilant care into polishing it to perfection.
38.29: Like the potter sitting at his work, and turning the wheel with his feet: constantly he is concerned with his work and all his efforts are aimed at producing a certain number of vases.
38.30: With his arm he shapes the clay and before his feet he makes it flexible, he puts his heart into perfecting the glaze, and takes vigilant care to clean his kiln.
38.31: These kinds of people expect everything from their own hands, and each of them is skilled in their own craft.
38.32: Without them we would not build any cities, we would not go abroad, we would not travel.
38.33: But they will not be sought in the council of the people and they will not distinguish themselves in the assembly, they will not have the knowledge of the alliance of law, they will not take their place on the seat of the judge, they will not interpret justice and law and they will not be found to pronounce sentences.
38.34: However, they support the things of the time and their prayer relates to the work of their trade.

39.1: It is different for the one who applies his mind and meditates on the law of the Most High. He seeks the wisdom of all the ancients and devotes his leisure to prophecies.
39.2: He keeps in his memory the stories of famous men and he delves into the intricacies of their sayings.
39.3: He seeks the hidden meaning of proverbs and he deals with enigmatic sayings.
39.4: He serves among the great and appears before the prince. He travels to the land of foreign peoples, for he wants to know good and evil among men.
39.5: He sets his heart to go early in the morning to the Lord who made him: he prays in the presence of the Most High, he opens his mouth for prayer and he asks forgiveness for his sins.
39.6: If it is the will of the Lord, who is great, he will be filled with the spirit of understanding, then he will pour out words of wisdom in abundance, and in his prayer he will give thanks to the Lord.
39.7: He will know how to direct his prudence and his knowledge, and he will study the divine mysteries.
39.8: He will publish the doctrine of his teaching and he will boast of the law of the covenant of the Lord.
39.9: Many will praise his intelligence and he will never be forgotten, his memory will not fade and his name will live on from age to age.
39.10: The people will recount his wisdom and the assembly will celebrate his praises.
39.11: As long as he lives, his name remains more illustrious than a thousand others, and when he rests, his glory will grow even more.
39.12: I still want to publish the fruit of my reflections, because I am filled, like the moon in its fullness.
39.13: Listen to me, pious sons, and grow, like the rose on the bank of a flowing stream:
39.14: Spread your sweet fragrance like incense, let your flower bloom like the lily, exhale your perfume and sing a song, praise the Lord for all his works.
39.15: Give glory to his name, proclaim his praise, with songs from your lips and on your harps, and in celebrating him you will say:
39.16: All the works of the Lord are very good, and everything he has commanded will be accomplished in its time.
39.17: We should not say, "What is this? What is its purpose?" for everything will be sought in its time. At his word, the waters gathered together like a heap, and at his word there were reservoirs of water.
39.18: By his command, what pleases him comes to pass, and no one can stop the salvation he sends.
39.19: The works of all flesh are before him, and one cannot hide from his sight.
39.20: His gaze reaches from eternity to eternity, and there is nothing astonishing in his presence.
39.21: We should not say, "What is this? What is it used for?" because everything has been created for its purpose.
39.22: The blessing of the Lord overflows like a river, and like a flood it waters the earth.
39.23: Likewise, he shares his wrath with the nations, as he turned a well-watered land into a land of salt.
39.24: His ways are right for the holy, but likewise they are stumbling blocks for the wicked.
39.25: Good things were created for the good from the beginning, and likewise, evils for the wicked.
39.26: The essentials for human life are water, fire, iron and salt, wheat flour, honey and milk, grape blood, oil and clothing.
39.27: All these things are good for pious men, but turn into evil for sinners.
39.28: There are winds created for vengeance, and in their fury, they unleash their plagues. On the day of destruction, they will display their power and appease the wrath of the One who made them.
39.29: Fire and hail, famine and plague, all these things were created for punishment,
39.30: as well as the teeth of ferocious beasts, scorpions and vipers, and the deadly sword of extermination against the wicked.
39.31: These creatures rejoice in the Lord's command, they stand ready on earth for need, and at the appointed times, they do not disobey his orders.
39.32: That is why I was firm in my thoughts from the beginning and, after meditating, I put them in writing:
39.33: All the works of the Lord are good, and he provides for every need in his time.
39.34: There is no reason to say, "This is worse than that," because everything will be recognized as good in its time.
39.35: And now sing with all your heart and mouth and bless the name of the Lord.

40.1: To every man has been imposed great misery, and a heavy yoke is upon the children of men, from the day they come forth from their mother's womb until the day they are buried in the bosom of the common mother.
40.2: What troubles their thoughts and makes their hearts fear is the thought of their expectation, the fear of death.
40.3: From the man who sits on a throne in glory, to the wretch sitting on the ground in ashes,
40.4: from the one who wears purple and a crown, to the wretch covered in coarse cloth,
40.5: Anger, envy, turmoil, restlessness, fear of death, bitterness and quarrels are the lot of all and, in the time when each one rests on his bed, the sleep of night disturbs his thoughts.
40.6: He rests for a moment, so little as to be nothing, and immediately dreams agitate him; he seems to be on sentry duty during the day, frightened by the vision in his mind, like a man fleeing from battle.
40.7: At the moment of deliverance, he wakes up and is astonished by his futile fear.
40.8: So it is with all flesh, from man to beast, and for sinners seven times more.
40.9: Plague, murder, discord, the sword, [calamities, famine, destruction and other scourges]:
40.10: All of this was created against sinners, just as it was because of them that the flood came.
40.11: Everything that comes from the earth returns to the earth, as everything that comes from the waters goes down to the sea.
40.12: Every gift and every unjustly acquired possession will perish, but good faith will endure forever.
40.13: The wealth of the unjust will dry up like a torrent and like a loud thunderclap during a downpour.
40.14: The man who knows how to open his hand will rejoice, but the wrongdoers will suffer total ruin.
40.15: The offspring of the wicked do not put forth many branches, and the unclean roots are on the steep rock.
40.16: The reed that grows near water and on the bank of a river is pulled up before any other grass.
40.17: Kindness is like a blessed garden, and benevolence remains forever.
40.18: Sweet is the life of the self-sufficient man, and sweeter than both of the worker's life, the life of the one who finds a treasure:
40.19: Children and the founding of a city perpetuate a name: more than either, an irreproachable woman is valued.
40.20: Wine and music gladden the heart: more than either one, the love of wisdom.
40.21: The flute and the harp produce sweet sounds: more than just the two, a benevolent language.
40.22: Grace and beauty delight your eyes: more than either, the greenery of the fields.
40.23: The friend and the companion meet at certain times: more than the two, the woman with her husband.
40.24: Brothers and aid are for the time of affliction: more than both, charity delivers.
40.25: Gold and silver strengthen the feet: more than either of them, prudence is valued.
40.26: Wealth and strength exalt the heart, but more than either, the fear of the Lord. With the fear of the Lord, one lacks nothing, and with it, there is no need to cry for help.
40.27: The fear of the Lord is like a blessed garden, and the Lord clothes it with unparalleled glory.
40.28: My son, may you not lead a life of begging; it is better to die than to beg.
40.29: When a man is reduced to looking towards another's table, his life cannot be counted as a life. For he defiles his soul with foreign food, something that a learned and well-bred man will avoid.
40.30: In the mouth of a shameless man, begging is sweet, but in his gut, it burns like a fire.

41.1: O death, how bitter is your memory to the man who lives in peace amidst his riches, to the man free from worries and who prospers in all things and who is still able to enjoy the pleasure of the table.
41.2: O death, your stopping is pleasant to the poor, to the one whose strength is exhausted, to the old man burdened with years and tormented by a thousand cares, to the one who does not submit to his fate and who has lost hope.
41.3: Do not fear the end of death: remember those who have gone before you and those who will come after.
41.4: This is the decree issued by the Lord on all flesh, and why rebel against the good pleasure of the Most High? Whether you have lived ten years, a hundred, a thousand: in Sheol, the length of life is no longer a concern.
41.5: The sons of sinners are abominable, for they frequent the dwellings of the wicked.
41.6: The inheritance of the children of sinners goes to ruin, and shame clings to their posterity.
41.7: The children of an ungodly father reproach him, because it is because of him that they are in disgrace.
41.8: Woe to you, ungodly men, who have forsaken the law of the Most High God.
41.9: If you are born, you are born into a curse, and if you die, the curse will be your portion.
41.10: Everything that is of the earth returns to the earth: so the wicked go from curse to ruin.
41.11: Men grieve for their bodies, but the hateful name of sinners will be destroyed.
41.12: Take care of your name, for it will remain with you longer than a thousand great treasures of gold.
41.13: We count the days of a good life, but a beautiful name remains forever.
41.14: My children, observe my instructions in peace: Hidden wisdom and invisible treasure, what good are they?
41.15: Better is a man who hides his foolishness than a man who hides his wisdom.
41.16: So be ashamed of the things I am about to tell you. For not all shame is good to keep, and not all things are judged by everyone according to the truth.
41.17: Be ashamed of fornication before your father and mother, and of lying before the prince and the powerful.,
41.18: of the offense before the judge and the magistrate, of the transgression of the law before the assembly and the people, of the injustice before the companion and the friend,
41.19: stealing in front of the neighbors. In the name of God's truth and his covenant, be ashamed to rest your elbow on the loaves, to incur contempt by taking and giving,
41.20: and to remain silent before those who greet you, to look at a debauched woman
41.21: and to turn away the face of a relative, to take away his share and his gift, and to watch a married woman,
41.22: to be familiar with your servant and do not stay near her bed, be ashamed of offensive words in front of your friends and do not reproach after you have given.

42.1: Be ashamed to repeat what you have heard and to reveal secrets. In this way you will be truly ashamed and find favor in the sight of all. Do not be ashamed of these things, and do not show favoritism to sin.
42.2: of the law of the Most High and of his covenant, of the sentence that justifies the wicked,
42.3: to converse with companions and passersby, to give some good to your friends,
42.4: to use fair scales and fair weights, to acquire much or little,
42.5: to show no favoritism in dealings with merchants, to discipline your children severely, and to beat a wicked slave until he bleeds.
42.6: With a wicked woman, the seal is good, and where there are many hands, put it under lock and key.
42.7: What you deliver to your people, count it and weigh it, and write down what you give and what you receive.
42.8: Do not be ashamed to rebuke the foolish, the simple-minded, or the old man who quarrels with young men. And in this way you will be truly instructed and approved by everyone.
42.9: A daughter is a secret worry for her father; the anxiety she causes him keeps him awake at night. As a young girl, she may spend the best of her life, but once married, she may become hateful to him.
42.10: As a virgin she could be seduced and become pregnant in her father's house, with a husband she might be unfaithful and live with him, perhaps she will remain sterile.
42.11: With regard to a headstrong girl, exercise severe vigilance lest she make you the laughingstock of your enemies, the laughingstock of the town and the subject of public gossip, and dishonor you in the midst of all the people.
42.12: Do not look at the beauty of any man, and do not sit among women.,
42.13: because from clothing comes the moth, and from woman comes feminine malice.
42.14: A wicked man is better than a caressing woman, for a woman brings shame and reproach.
42.15: I will recount the works of the Lord and proclaim what I have seen. It is by the word of the Lord that his works were done.
42.16: The sun that shines upon them contemplates them all, the work of the Lord, it is filled with his glory.
42.17: The Lord has not enabled his saints to proclaim all his wonders, which the Lord Almighty has firmly established, so that the whole universe may be established by his glory.
42.18: He searches the depths of the ocean and the hearts of men, and he knows their most subtle designs. For the Lord possesses all knowledge and he sees the signs of the times.
42.19: It announces the past and the future and it reveals the traces of hidden things.
42.20: No thought escapes him, no word is hidden from him.
42.21: He adorned the great works with his wisdom, he is before all ages and he will endure forever, nothing has been added to his being and nothing has been taken from it and he needed no advisor.
42.22: How beautiful all his works are. And what we contemplate of them is but a spark.
42.23: Everything is alive and remains forever, for all uses, and everything obeys the Creator.
42.24: Everything is in pairs, one individual facing another, and he hasn't done anything that would lead to ruin.
42.25: One ensures the happiness of the other: who can be satisfied with seeing the glory of the Lord?

43.1: The pride of the heights is the firmament in its pure splendor, and the appearance of the sky is a vision of glory.
43.2: The sun, when it appears, glorifies the Lord; at its rising, it is a marvelous creature, the work of the Most High.
43.3: At midday, it dries up the earth; who can withstand its heat?
43.4: For his work, the craftsman stoks the fire of the furnace: three times as hot as the sun heats the mountains. It ignites the vapors in the air and, when it shines its rays, it dazzles the eyes.
43.5: Great is the Lord who made it, and at his command he hastens his course.
43.6: The moon, too, is always faithful; at the time assigned to it, it indicates the times of the year and announces the future.
43.7: The moon signals the celebrations; its light diminishes when it reaches its fullness.
43.8: From her the month takes its name; she grows marvelously in her various phases. She is the tent of a camp in the heights of heaven; she shines in the firmament of the sky.
43.9: The beauty of the heavens is the splendor of the stars, a radiant adornment in the heights of the Lord.
43.10: According to the Saint's order, they stay in their assigned place and do not tire themselves out during their vigils.
43.11: See the rainbow and bless the One who made it; it is truly beautiful in its splendor.
43.12: He embraces the sky in his radiant circle; it is the hands of the Most High that extend him.
43.13: At his command, the snow rushes down and the lightning hurries forward, executors of his judgments.
43.14: That is why its treasures open and the clouds fly like birds.
43.15: By his power, he gives strength to the clouds and the hail falls like shards of stone.
43.16: The sound of his thunder makes the earth tremble, and when he appears, the mountains tremble.
43.17: At its will, the south wind blows, the north wind unleashes its fury, and the whirlwind rages. It scatters the snow like birds swooping down; it descends like a grasshopper pausing.
43.18: The eye admires the beauty of its whiteness and the heart is amazed by its fall.
43.19: It pours frost on the earth like salt, and the frost hardens it into thorny points.
43.20: The cold north wind begins to blow and the water hardens into ice; this ice spreads motionless over every mass of water and covers the water like a suit of armor.
43.21: The Lord devours the mountains and sets the desert ablaze; he burns the vegetation like fire.
43.22: The remedy for all these ills is a cloud that arrives quickly, a dew that comes to console from the burning heat.
43.23: According to his plan, he tamed the abyss and planted islands there.
43.24: Those who sail the sea tell of its perils, and when we hear their stories, we are amazed.
43.25: There are strange and wonderful creatures, animals of all kinds, and the race of sea monsters.
43.26: Through the Lord, all things happily come to their end, and all things subsist by his word.
43.27: We could say a lot and still not achieve it. The summary of our speech: He is everything.
43.28: If we wish to praise Him, where would we find the strength? He is the Almighty, superior to all His works.
43.29: The Lord is awesome, and supremely great, and marvelous is his power.
43.30: In praising the Lord, exalt him as much as you can, for he will always be higher still. To exalt him, gather all your strength; do not grow weary, for you will never reach him.
43.31: Who has seen it and could speak about it? Who is capable of praising it as it is?
43.32: Many hidden wonders are even greater, for we see only a small number of his works.
43.33: The Lord has done all things and he gives wisdom to the righteous.

44.1: Let us therefore praise the illustrious men and fathers of our race.
44.2: In them the Lord has worked glorious wonders; he has manifested his greatness from the beginning.
44.3: They were rulers in their kingdoms, men renowned for their power, wise counselors, announcing the divine will through their prophecies,
44.4: guides of the people through their advice and prudence, doctors of the people, who instructed them through wise speeches,
44.5: men who cultivated the art of sacred melodies and who committed poetic narratives to writing,
44.6: The wealthy, possessing abundant goods and living peacefully in their homes: all these men were honored by their contemporaries.,
44.7: They were all the glory of their time.
44.8: There are some among them who have left a name, so that their praises may be told.
44.9: There are some of whom there is no longer any memory; they perished as if they had never existed, they became as if they had never been born, and likewise, their children after them.
44.10: The first were pious men, whose virtues have not been forgotten.
44.11: Happiness remains attached to their race and an inheritance is assured to their children.
44.12: Their race remains faithful to the alliances, and their children because of them.
44.13: Their race remains forever and their glory will never be erased.
44.14: Their bodies were buried in peace, and their name lives on from age to age.
44.15: The people celebrate their wisdom and the assembly proclaims their praises.
44.16: Enoch was pleasing to the Lord and was taken up as an example of penitence for generations to come.
44.17: Noah was found to be perfect and righteous; in the time of wrath, he was the ransom for humanity. That is why a remnant was left to the earth when the flood came.
44.18: An eternal covenant was made with him, so that all flesh would never again be destroyed by a flood.
44.19: Abraham is the illustrious father of a multitude of nations, and no one has been found to equal him in glory.
44.20: He kept the law of the Most High and entered into a covenant with him. He established this covenant in his flesh and, in the trial, he proved faithful.
44.21: So God assured him by oath that the nations would be blessed in his offspring, he promised to multiply him like the dust of the earth, to raise his descendants like the stars, to give them as an inheritance from the sea to the other sea, from the river to the ends of the earth.
44.22: In the same way he confirmed in Isaac, because of his father Abraham, the blessing of all peoples and the covenant.
44.23: And he laid it upon Jacob’s head, and showed consideration for him in his blessings, and gave him the land as an inheritance, and made it into various portions and divided it among the twelve tribes.

45.1: He brought forth from Jacob a righteous man, who found favor with all people, a man loved by God and men: Moses. May his memory be a blessing.
45.2: He gave him a glory equal to that of the saints, he made him great by the terrors he inspired in his enemies.
45.3: By his word, he put an end to the wonders, he glorified him before kings, he gave him commandments for his people, and he showed him a ray of his glory.
45.4: Because of his faith and his mercy, he consecrated him, he chose him from among all mortals.
45.5: He made him hear his voice and brought him into the cloud, he gave him face to face the commandments, the law of life and of knowledge, so that he might teach Jacob his covenant and his decrees to Israel.
45.6: He raised up Aaron, a holy man like himself, his brother, from the tribe of Levi.
45.7: He made an everlasting covenant with him and appointed him priests over his people. He adorned him with splendid regalia and girded him with a robe of glory.
45.8: He clothed him in sovereign magnificence and assigned him clothes of honor: the breeches, the long tunic and the ephod.
45.9: He surrounded it with pomegranates, with many small golden bells around it, which were to ring when he walked and make their sound heard in the temple; it was a memorial for the sons of his people.
45.10: He clothed him with the sacred garment, woven of gold, hyacinth, and purple, the work of an embroiderer, the rational of judgment, with the Urim and Thummim, made of scarlet threads, the work of an artist,
45.11: with precious stones, engraved like seals and set in gold, the work of a lapidary, to be a memorial, names being written, according to the number of the tribes of Israel.
45.12: He placed on her the golden crown, bearing these engraved words: Holy of the Lord, emblem of honor, perfect work, delight of the eyes, magnificent adornment.
45.13: Nothing like it existed before him and nothing like it ever will; no stranger has ever worn it, but only his sons and descendants throughout all ages.
45.14: His holocausts will be offered twice a day, without interruption.
45.15: Moses filled his hands and anointed him with holy oil. This was an everlasting covenant for him and his descendants, as long as heaven lasts, to serve the Lord, to fulfill the priestly duties, and to bless his people in his name.
45.16: The Lord chose him from among all the living, to present to him the offering, the incense, and the sweet fragrance as a memorial portion and to make atonement for the sins of his people.
45.17: He gave him authority over the holy ordinances in his commandments, to teach Jacob his precepts, and to instruct Israel in his law.
45.18: Foreigners conspired against him and were jealous of him in the desert: the men of the party of Dathan and Abiram and the band of Korah, ardent and furious.
45.19: The Lord saw it and was not pleased, and they were destroyed in the fierceness of his anger; he performed wonders against them and consumed them with the flame of his fire.
45.20: And he increased Aaron’s glory and assigned him an inheritance: he gave him the firstfruits of the fruits of the earth, first of all he prepared bread to satisfy their hunger.
45.21: They feed on the sacrifices of the Lord, which he gave to Aaron and his descendants.
45.22: But he has no inheritance in the land of the people and no share in the nation, for, says the Lord, «I will be your portion and your inheritance.»
45.23: Phinehas, son of Eleazar, is the third in glory, in that he showed zeal in the fear of the Lord and that, in the defection of the people, he remained firm in the noble courage of his soul and made atonement for Israel.
45.24: Therefore, a peace alliance was concluded with him, which made him leader of the priests and of his people, so that to him and his descendants the august dignity of the priesthood would forever belong.
45.25: There was also an alliance with David, son of Jesse, of the tribe of Judah; the king's inheritance passes only from son to son, while Aaron's inheritance belongs to all his descendants.
45.26: May the Lord give you, O high priests, wisdom in your hearts, to judge his people in righteousness, so that their prosperity may not perish, nor their glory in future ages.

46.1: He was valiant in war, Joshua son of Nun, who succeeded Moses in the dignity of prophet and who, verifying his name, showed himself great in the deliverance of the Lord's chosen ones, by punishing the rebellious enemies, in order to put Israel in possession of the land.
46.2: What glory he covered himself with when he raised his hands and stretched out his sword against the cities.
46.3: Who before him ever endured so many battles, when the Lord himself brought the enemies?
46.4: Did not the sun, at the gesture of his hand, go back in time, and was not one day like two days?
46.5: He invoked the Most High Sovereign while he pressed the enemies on all sides and the Almighty Lord heard him, with hailstones of great force.
46.6: The Lord descended upon the hostile nation and destroyed the adversaries in the defile, so that the nations might know all the weapons of Joshua and learn that the war he was waging was before the Lord, for he followed the Almighty.
46.7: Already in the days of Moses, he and Caleb, son of Jephone, had shown his piety by standing firm against the enemy, preventing the people from sinning, and silencing the murmuring of the wicked.
46.8: Thus, these two men alone were preserved, out of six hundred thousand foot soldiers, to be brought into the inheritance, into the land flowing with milk and honey.
46.9: And the Lord gave Caleb strength, and it remained with him until old age, so that he was able to go up to the heights of the region, and his descendants preserved this inheritance:
46.10: so that all the children of Israel may recognize that it is good to follow the Lord.
46.11: And the Judges, each according to his name, all those whose hearts did not give themselves over to fornication, all those who did not turn away from the Lord, may their memory be a blessing.
46.12: May their bones bloom again from the bosom of their tomb, may their name be renewed in the children of these illustrious men.
46.13: Samuel was loved by the Lord his God; a prophet of the Lord, he established the kingdom and anointed princes to rule over his people.
46.14: He judged the assembly according to the law of the Lord, and the Lord looked down on Jacob.
46.15: By his truthfulness he proved himself a prophet; by his truthfulness a trustworthy seer was recognized.
46.16: He invoked the sovereign Lord when his enemies pressed him on all sides and he offered a lamb still at the breast.
46.17: And the Lord thundered from heaven, and with great splendor he uttered his voice,
46.18: and he crushed the leaders of the Tyrians and all the princes of the Philistines.
46.19: Before the time of eternal sleep, Samuel called upon the Lord and his Anointed to witness: "From no one I have received no gift, not even sandals," and no man stood up to accuse him.
46.20: And when he had fallen asleep, he prophesied, and announced to the king his imminent end. From the heart of the earth, he raised his voice in prophesying, in order to blot out the iniquity of the people.

47.1: Then Nathan appeared, to prophesy in the days of David.
47.2: Just as the fat is separated from the thanksgiving offering, so David was set apart among the children of Israel.
47.3: He played with the lions as with young goats, with the bears as with young lambs.
47.4: In his youth, did he not kill the giant, and remove the people's disgrace, when he raised his hand with the stone of his sling and struck down the insolence of Goliath?
47.5: For he called upon the Lord Most High, and the Lord gave strength to his right hand, to slay the mighty warrior, to exalt the horn of his people.
47.6: Therefore he was celebrated because of the ten thousand, he was praised because of the blessings of the Lord, and he was given the crown of glory.
47.7: For he crushed the enemies on all sides, he trampled the Philistines underfoot, his adversaries, he broke their power to this day.
47.8: In all his undertakings, he paid homage to the Holy One, the Most High, in words of praise, with all his heart, he sang hymns and he loved the One who had made him.
47.9: He appointed singers before the altar and, through their voices, he made sweet melodies heard.
47.10: He gave brilliance to the feasts and sovereign splendor to the solemnities, while the singers celebrated the holy name of the Lord and, from the morning, made the sanctuary resound.
47.11: The Lord forgave him his sins and raised up his power forever, and assured him a line of kings and a throne of glory in Israel.
47.12: After him arose a son full of wisdom; because of his father, he lived in prosperity.
47.13: Solomon reigned in days of peace, God having granted him rest all around, so that he might build a temple for his name and prepare an eternal sanctuary.
47.14: How wise you were in your youth, and overflowing with understanding, like a river.
47.15: Your spirit has covered the earth and filled it with hidden meanings.
47.16: Your name reached the distant islands and you were loved in your peace.
47.17: For your hymns, your proverbs, your parables, and your answers, the world has admired you.
47.18: In the name of the Lord God, who is called the God of Israel, you have amassed gold like tin and heaped up silver like lead.
47.19: You gave yourself over to women and gave power over your body.,
47.20: You have stained your glory and defiled your race, and thus you have brought wrath upon your children. I feel a cruel pain for your folly.,
47.21: It was the cause that the empire was divided in two and that from Ephraim arose the leader of a rebellious kingdom.
47.22: But the Lord will not abandon his mercy, and none of his works will perish. He will not destroy the descendants of his chosen one, nor will he wipe out the offspring of his beloved. He has left Jacob a remnant, and David a descendant of his own.
47.23: Solomon rested with his fathers and left after him, from his descendants, a son, foolish in the eyes of the people, lacking in prudence, Rehoboam, who led the people to stray from his counsels, and Jeroboam, son of Nebat, who caused Israel to sin and opened the way to Ephraim for transgression.
47.24: And the sins of the Israelites multiplied exceedingly, so that they were taken away from their country.
47.25: They pursued all kinds of iniquities, until vengeance came upon them.

48.1: Then Elijah arose, a prophet like fire, and his word was ablaze like a torch.
48.2: He brought famine upon Israel and, through his zeal, reduced them to a small number.
48.3: By the word of the Lord, he shut up the heavens, and likewise, he caused fire to fall from them three times.
48.4: How glorious you have become, O Elijah, by your wonders, and who could boast of being like you?
48.5: You who raised a corpse from the depths of death and Sheol, by the word of the Most High,
48.6: You who have plunged kings into ruin and illustrious figures from their beds into death,
48.7: You who have heard rebukes on Sinai and decrees of vengeance on Horeb,
48.8: You who have anointed kings to carry out vengeance and prophets to succeed you,
48.9: you who were caught up in a whirlwind of flame and in a chariot with horses of fire,
48.10: you who have been appointed in severe writings for times to come, as to appease the anger before it flares up, to turn the heart of the father to the child, and to restore the tribes of Israel.
48.11: Blessed are those who see you and are clothed with the love of God. For we too will surely live.
48.12: When Elijah was caught in the whirlwind, Elisha was filled with his spirit. During his days, he was not shaken by any prince and no one overpowered him.
48.13: Nothing was impossible for him, and his body, lying in the tomb, performed miracles.
48.14: During his life he performed wonders, and in his death he worked marvels.
48.15: Despite all this, the people did not repent and did not turn away from sin, until they were taken far from their land and scattered throughout the earth. Only a small people remained, with a leader from the house of David.
48.16: Among them, some did what was pleasing to God, and others multiplied transgressions.
48.17: Hezekiah fortified his city and brought the Gihon into its walls; with iron, he carved out the rock and built reservoirs for the water.
48.18: In his time Sennacherib went up and sent Rabsaces, who went and raised his hand against Zion and boasted in his boasting.
48.19: Then their hearts and their hands trembled, and they were in pain like women giving birth.
48.20: They called upon the merciful Lord, stretching out their hands to Him, and the Holy One heard them at once from heaven and delivered them through the ministry of Isaiah.
48.21: He struck down the Assyrian army and his angel exterminated them.
48.22: For Hezekiah did what was pleasing to the Lord and stood firm in the ways of David his father, which Isaiah the prophet, great and true in his visions, commanded him.
48.23: During his days, the sun went back in time, and Isaiah prolonged the king's life.
48.24: Inspired by a powerful vision, he saw the times to come and comforted the afflicted in Zion. He announced what must happen until the end of time and the hidden things before their fulfillment.

49.1: The memory of Josias is a perfume composed of sweet scents, prepared by the art of the perfumer; in every mouth, his memory is sweet as honey and like music at a feast.
49.2: He succeeded in bringing the nation to repentance and he eliminated the abominations of impiety.
49.3: He faithfully turned his heart to the Lord and, in the days of the wicked, he strengthened piety.
49.4: With the exception of David, Hezekiah, and Josiah, all the others committed iniquity, for, having abandoned the law of the Most High, the kings of Judah went to their ruin.
49.5: Indeed, they left their power and glory to a foreign nation.
49.6: They burned the chosen city of the sanctuary and left its squares deserted.,
49.7: because of Jeremiah, for they mistreated him, consecrated prophet from his mother's womb, to overthrow, destroy and destroy, as well as to build and plant.
49.8: Ezekiel beheld the vision of glory, which the Lord showed him upon the chariot of the Cherubim,
49.9: for he considered the enemies in the threat of a storm and he did good to those who followed the straight path.
49.10: As for the twelve prophets, may their bones rise again from their graves. For they comforted Jacob and saved him with a sure hope.
49.11: How should we celebrate Zerubbabel? For he is like a signet ring on the right hand.
49.12: So it was with Jesus, son of Josedec. Both of them, in their days, rebuilt the house of God and restored the temple, consecrated to the Lord, destined for eternal glory.
49.13: Nehemiah is also remembered greatly, he who restored our ruined walls, who reinstated our gates with their bars and rebuilt our houses.
49.14: No man has existed on earth like Enoch, for he too was taken from this earth.
49.15: No man was like Joseph, the prince of his brothers, the support of his nation, and his bones were carefully preserved.
49.16: Shem and Seth were glorified among men, but above all beings in creation is Adam.

50.1: Simon, son of Onias, was the high priest who, during his lifetime, repaired the house of the Lord, and, during his days, strengthened the temple.
50.2: He laid the foundations to double the height of the high wall that supports the temple enclosure.
50.3: In his time the water reservoir was made; the bronze from which it was formed had the perimeter of the sea.
50.4: He took care of his people to preserve them from ruin and fortified the city against a siege.
50.5: How majestic he was amidst the people gathered all around, when he came out of the House of the Veil.
50.6: He was like the morning star that shines through the cloud, like the moon on its full days,
50.7: like the sun that shines brightly on the temple of the Most High, and like the rainbow that shines amidst the luminous clouds,
50.8: like the rose in springtime, like the lilies by the water's edge, like the fragrant branch of the tree in summertime,
50.9: like perfume on the censer fire, like a solid gold vessel adorned with all kinds of precious stones,
50.10: like the olive tree that bears its fruit and like the cypress that rises into the clouds.
50.11: When he had taken on the robe of honor and donned all his ornaments and ascended to the holy altar, he made the surroundings of the sanctuary shine brightly.
50.12: But when he received the parts of the victims from the hands of the priests and stood near the hearth of the altar, his brothers forming a crown around him, then he appeared like a majestic cedar over Lebanon and the priests surrounded him like palm trunks.
50.13: All of Aaron's sons were clothed in their magnificent ornaments and they held in their hands the offering for the Lord, before all the assembly of Israel.
50.14: And when he had finished the service on the altars, in order to beautify the offering of the Most High Almighty, he would extend his hand over the libation cup and pour out the blood of the grape.
50.15: He poured it on the base of the altar, a pleasing fragrance to the Most High, the great King.
50.16: Then Aaron's sons shouted, they sounded their trumpets of beaten metal and made loud cries, in remembrance before the Most High.
50.17: And all the people at once hastened and fell face down to the ground, to worship their Lord, the Almighty God, the Most High.
50.18: And the singers, raising their voices, praised him; the vast temple resounded with sweet harmonies.
50.19: And the people beseeched the Lord most loudly, standing in prayer before the Merciful One, until the ceremony of the Lord was completed and the priests had performed the sacred functions.
50.20: Then the high priest would come down and raise his hand over the whole assembly of the children of Israel, to give with his lips the blessing of the Lord and to glorify himself in his name.
50.21: And the people prostrated themselves again to receive the blessing from the Most High.
50.22: And now, bless the Lord of the universe, who does great things everywhere, who has exalted our days from the beginning and has dealt with us according to his mercy.
50.23: May He grant us joy of heart and may peace be in Israel today as in days of the past.
50.24: May His mercy remain with us forever, and may He deliver us when His day comes.
50.25: There are two nations that my soul hates, and the third is not even a nation:
50.26: those who dwell in the mountains of Seir, the Philistines, and the foolish people who live in Shechem.
50.27: I have recorded in this book a teaching of understanding and knowledge, I, Jesus, son of Sirach, of Jerusalem, who poured out the wisdom of my heart.
50.28: Blessed is he who devotes himself to these teachings. He who gathers them in his heart will become wise.,
50.29: for if he puts them into practice, he will triumph over everything, because the light of the Lord is his path.

51.1: I want to celebrate you, Lord, King, I want to praise you, O God and Savior, I celebrate your name.
51.2: For you have been my protector and helper; you have delivered my body from ruin, from the snare of the slanderous tongue, from the lips of those who practice lies, and from the face of my adversaries,
51.3: You have been my support and you have delivered me, according to the greatness of your mercy and your name, from those who gnashed their teeth, ready to devour me, from the hand of those who sought my life, from all the tribulations with which I was besieged,
51.4: from the suffocating heat of the fire that surrounded me, from the midst of a fire I hadn't lit,
51.5: from the deep abyss of Sheol, from the unclean tongue and the lying word spoken to the king, from the slander of an unjust tongue.
51.6: My soul was approaching death and my life was touching Sheol below.
51.7: They surrounded me on all sides and there was no one to help me; I looked for help from men and there was none.
51.8: Then I remembered your mercy, Lord, and your works of old, I remembered that you saved those who hoped in you and delivered them from the hands of idolatrous nations.
51.9: And, prostrate on the ground, I offered up my prayer and begged you to save me from death.
51.10: I called upon the Lord, the father of my Lord, that he would not abandon me in the days of my distress, in the time of the proud, when there was no help:
51.11: «I will praise your name forever and sing it in gratitude.» And my prayer was answered.,
51.12: For you have saved me from ruin and delivered me in times of trouble. Therefore I will praise you and extol you and bless the name of the Lord.
51.13: When I was still young, before I had gone astray, I openly sought wisdom in my prayer.
51.14: I asked for it in front of the temple and I will search for it until the end.
51.15: Seeing its blossom, as at the sight of the ripening grapes, my heart rejoiced in it. With it, my foot has walked on the right path; from my youth, I have followed in its footsteps.
51.16: I listened to her for a while and took it in, and I found it a great lesson for me.
51.17: Thanks to her, I have gained a great profit: to him who gave me wisdom I want to give glory.
51.18: For I have resolved to put it into practice, I have applied myself to doing good, and I will not be put to shame.
51.19: For her my soul struggled and I took great care in my actions. I raised my hands above and regretted having ignored her.
51.20: Towards her I directed my soul and, in the purity of life, I found her. With her, from the beginning, I acquired understanding, therefore I will never be abandoned.
51.21: My very being stirred in my search for it, and so I acquired a precious treasure.
51.22: The Lord has given me the gift of speech as a reward, and I will use it to praise Him.
51.23: Come near to me, you ignorant ones, and make your home in the house of instruction.,
51.24: because you lack wisdom and your souls thirst greatly.
51.25: I open my mouth and speak: Get it for free,
51.26: Bend your neck under the yoke and let your soul receive instruction; you do not have to go far to find it.
51.27: See for yourselves that I have worked little and have found great rest.
51.28: Take part in education at a high price of money, and with it you will have acquired abundant gold.
51.29: Let your soul rejoice in the mercy of the Lord and not be ashamed of his praise.
51.30: Complete your work before the time, and in due time, he will give you the reward.
